title.zh: 卷五十一 賈鄒枚路傳
title.en: Volume 51: Jia, Zou, Mei and Lu
title.raw: 卷五十一 賈鄒枚路傳 Volume 51: Jia, Zou, Mei and Lu

sentences.zh: 卷五十一 賈鄒枚路傳第二十一

translations.literal: Volume 51: Biographies of Jia, Zou, Mei, and Lu, number twenty-one.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 51: Biographies of Jia, Zou, Mei, and Lu, the twenty-first scroll.

sentences.zh: 賈山，穎川人也。

translations.literal: Jia Shan was a man of Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Shan came from Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 祖父祛，故魏王時博士弟子也。

translations.literal: His grandfather Qu had been a doctoral disciple in the time of the former King of Wei.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather Qu had been a doctoral student in the days of the former King of Wei.

sentences.zh: 山受學祛，所言涉獵書記，不能為醇儒。

translations.literal: Shan received instruction from Qu; what he spoke of roamed through documents and records, and he could not become a pure Confucian scholar.
translations.idiomatic: Shan studied under Qu; his learning ranged widely over writings and records, so he never became a narrow, textbook Confucian.

sentences.zh: 嘗給事穎陰侯為騎。

translations.literal: He once served the Marquis of Yingyin as a mounted attendant.
translations.idiomatic: He once served as a mounted attendant to the Marquis of Yingyin.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，言治亂之道，借秦為諭，名曰《至言》。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, he spoke on the way of order and disorder, taking Qin as a parable, and entitled it "The Utmost Words."
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen he set forth how states rise and fall, using Qin as his cautionary tale, in a work he called "Words to the Utmost".

sentences.zh: 其辭曰：

translations.literal: Its text says:
translations.idiomatic: The piece begins:

sentences.zh: 臣聞為人臣者，盡忠竭愚，以直諫主，不避死亡之誅者，臣山是也。

translations.literal: I have heard that one who is a minister to others exhausts loyalty and pours out foolishness, remonstrating with his lord by straight speaking and not shrinking from the punishment of death—such a minister is I, Shan.
translations.idiomatic: They say a good minister gives his ruler honest counsel even at the cost of his life; Your Majesty, that minister is myself, Shan.

sentences.zh: 臣不敢以久遠諭，願借秦以為諭，唯陛下少加意焉。

translations.literal: Your servant does not dare use remote antiquity as a parable; I beg to take Qin as a parable; may Your Majesty slightly lend his mind to it.
translations.idiomatic: I will not weary you with distant ages; let Qin be my mirror. I ask only that Your Majesty give this a moment’s careful thought.

sentences.zh: 夫布衣韋帶之士，修身於內，成名於外，而使後世不絕息。

translations.literal: As for the scholar in plain cloth and leather belt, he cultivates himself within and makes a name without, and causes later generations not to break off their breath.
translations.idiomatic: The man in homespun and a leather belt polishes his character at home and wins repute abroad, and his line does not die out.

sentences.zh: 至秦則不然。

translations.literal: When it came to Qin it was not so.
translations.idiomatic: Under Qin, nothing of the kind was true.

sentences.zh: 貴為天子，富有天下，賦斂重數，百姓任罷，赭衣半道，群盜滿山，使天下之人戴目而視，傾耳而聽。

translations.literal: Honored as Son of Heaven, rich as possessor of the empire, levies and exactions repeated and heavy, the common people worn out and exhausted, convicts in russet filling half the roads, bandits filling the mountains, causing the people of the empire to stare with eyes and strain with ears.
translations.idiomatic: Qin’s ruler sat as Son of Heaven and owned the world, yet he piled tax after tax on the people until they were broken, russet-clad convicts choked the highways, and robbers swarmed the hills; everyone in the realm watched and listened in dread.

sentences.zh: 一夫大呼，天下響應者，陳勝是也。

translations.literal: When one man gave a great shout and the empire answered him in echo—that was Chen Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: When one man raised a cry and the whole realm answered—that was Chen Sheng.

sentences.zh: 秦非徒如此也，起咸陽而西至雍，離宮三百，鐘鼓帷帳，不移而具。

translations.literal: Qin did not stop at this: from Xianyang west to Yong, three hundred detached palaces, bells, drums, curtains, and hangings—without moving they were all supplied.
translations.idiomatic: Nor did Qin stop there: from Xianyang west to Yong stood three hundred detached palaces, complete with bells and drums, curtains and canopies, all kept ready without ever being visited.

sentences.zh: 又為阿房之殿，殿高數十仞，東西五里，南北千步，從車羅騎，四馬鶩馳，旌旗不橈。

translations.literal: Again they built the Epang Palace hall; the hall rose several tens of ren high, five li from east to west, a thousand paces from south to north; attendant chariots and riders in rows, four-horse teams galloping like wild ducks, banners and flags unfurled.
translations.idiomatic: They raised the halls of Epang—scores of ren in height, five li east to west and a thousand paces north to south—with chariots and riders massed in ranks, four-horse teams racing like wildfowl, and banners that never furled.

sentences.zh: 為宮室之麗至於此，使其後世曾不得聚廬而托處焉。

translations.literal: The splendor of the palace chambers reached this point, so that his later generations could not even gather a hut and lodge in it.
translations.idiomatic: Palace splendor went so far that his descendants could not have found room to pitch a single hut among it all.

sentences.zh: 為馳道於天下，東窮燕、齊，南極吳、楚，江湖之上，瀕海之觀畢至。

translations.literal: They made post roads through the empire, east exhausting Yan and Qi, south reaching Wu and Chu; on the Yangtze and the lakes, and every seaside vista, all arrived.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial highways ran to every corner—east to Yan and Qi, south to Wu and Chu—until every famous view along the great rivers and the coast was stitched into one network.

sentences.zh: 道廣五十步，三丈而樹，厚築其外，隱以金椎，樹以青松。

translations.literal: The road was fifty paces wide; every three zhang they planted trees, thickly rammed the outside, hidden with metal mallets, planted with green pines.
translations.idiomatic: Each road was fifty paces across; pines were planted every three zhang, the embankments rammed solid and studded with metal pegs.

sentences.zh: 為馳道之麗至於此，使其後世曾不得邪徑而托足焉。

translations.literal: The splendor of the post roads reached this point, so that his later generations could not even take a bypath and set foot on them.
translations.idiomatic: The highways were glorified to a point where later generations could hardly set foot on a side path.

sentences.zh: 死葬乎驪山，吏徒數十萬人，曠日十年。

translations.literal: Buried at Mount Li, officials and convicts several tens of thousands, days stretching into ten years.
translations.idiomatic: His tomb at Mount Li consumed tens of thousands of laborers and convicts for ten years on end.

sentences.zh: 下徹三泉合采金石，冶銅錮其內，□塗其外，被以珠玉，飾以翡翠，中成觀游，上成山林，為葬□之侈至於此，使其後世曾不得蓬顆蔽塚而托葬焉。

translations.literal: They bored down to the three springs and joined metal and stone, smelted copper to seal the inner coffin, [lacuna] smeared the outside, covered it with pearls and jade, ornamented it with kingfisher feathers; in the middle it became a viewing tower, above it became forests and hills; the extravagance of the burial reached this point, so that his later generations could not even get a clod of earth over a grave mound and be buried in it.
translations.idiomatic: They sank shafts to the three underground streams, fused copper to line the vault, and sealed the outer shell with cinnabar lacquer, then draped the work in pearls and jade and kingfisher inlay until the middle was a pleasure gallery and the top a miniature forest; burial luxury went so far that later generations could not have afforded even a pebble’s shelter for their own graves.

sentences.zh: 秦以熊羆之力，虎狼之心，蠶食諸侯，併吞海內，而不篤禮義，故天殃已加矣。

translations.literal: Qin, with the strength of bears and brown bears and the heart of tigers and wolves, nibbled away at the feudal lords and swallowed the seas within, yet did not honor ritual and righteousness; therefore Heaven’s calamity had already been added.
translations.idiomatic: Qin used brute force and a predator’s appetite to pick off the nobles and swallow the realm, all the while spurning ritual and right—so Heaven’s punishment was already upon it.

sentences.zh: 臣昧死以聞，願陛下少留意而詳擇其中。

translations.literal: Your servant risks death to report this; I hope Your Majesty will slightly take note and carefully choose among them.
translations.idiomatic: I risk my life to lay this before you; I beg Your Majesty to weigh it with care.

sentences.zh: 臣聞忠臣之事君也，言切直則不用而身危，不切直則不可以明道，故切直之言，明主所欲急聞，忠臣之所以蒙死而竭知也。

translations.literal: I have heard that when a loyal minister serves his lord, if his words are cutting and straight they are not used and his person is in danger; if they are not cutting and straight he cannot clarify the Way; therefore cutting and straight words are what an enlightened ruler desires urgently to hear, and why a loyal minister covers death and exhausts his knowledge.
translations.idiomatic: A loyal minister’s dilemma is this: blunt truth may cost him his life, yet softened speech cannot illuminate the Way. That is why a wise ruler hungers for blunt counsel, and why a true minister will face death to give it.

sentences.zh: 地之磽者，雖有善種，不能生焉；

translations.literal: As for land that is stony, though there be good seed, it cannot grow there.
translations.idiomatic: On barren ground even the finest seed will not take.

sentences.zh: 江皋河瀕，雖有惡種，無不猥大。

translations.literal: On riverbanks and river margins, though there be bad seed, none do not grow rank and large.
translations.idiomatic: Along riverbanks even poor seed grows thick and tall.

sentences.zh: 昔者夏、商之季世，雖關龍逢、箕子、比干之賢，身死亡而道不用。

translations.literal: In the last ages of Xia and Shang, though there were the worthies Guan Longpeng, Jizi, and Bigan, their persons died and the Way was not employed.
translations.idiomatic: At the fall of Xia and Shang, men as worthy as Guan Longpeng, Jizi, and Bigan perished while their counsel went unused.

sentences.zh: 文王之時，豪俊之士皆得竭其智，芻蕘采薪之人皆得盡其力，此周之所以興也。

translations.literal: In the time of King Wen, heroic men all obtained to exhaust their wisdom, and even firewood-gatherers could exhaust their strength; this is why Zhou rose.
translations.idiomatic: Under King Wen every able man could offer his wit and even the woodcutter his strength—that is how the house of Zhou flourished.

sentences.zh: 故地之美者善養禾，君之仁者善養士。

translations.literal: Therefore beautiful land is good at nourishing grain; a humane lord is good at nourishing scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Rich soil feeds good grain; a humane ruler feeds good men.

sentences.zh: 雷霆之所擊，無不摧折者；

translations.literal: Where thunderbolts strike, nothing is not shattered and broken.
translations.idiomatic: What the thunderbolt strikes is shattered.

sentences.zh: 萬鈞之所壓，無不糜滅者。

translations.literal: Where the weight of ten thousand jun presses, nothing is not crushed to nothing.
translations.idiomatic: What ten thousand jun crushes is ground to dust.

sentences.zh: 今人主之威，非特雷霆也；

translations.literal: Now the awe of the human lord is not only thunderbolts.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler’s majesty is more terrible than any thunderbolt.

sentences.zh: 勢重，非特萬鈞也。

translations.literal: The weight of his power is not only ten thousand jun.
translations.idiomatic: The weight of his authority exceeds any ten thousand jun.

sentences.zh: 開道而求諫，和顏色而受之，用其言而顯其身，士猶恐懼而不敢自盡，又乃況於縱欲恣行暴虐，惡聞其過乎！

translations.literal: If he opens the way and seeks remonstrance, receives it with harmonious countenance, employs their words and makes their persons illustrious, scholars still fear and dare not exhaust themselves—how much the more if he indulges his desires, acts violently and cruelly, and hates to hear of his faults.
translations.idiomatic: Even when a ruler invites advice with an open road and a kindly face, and promotes those who speak truly, men still tremble and hold back—let alone when he gives free rein to cruelty and loathes any word of his faults.

sentences.zh: 震之以威，壓之以重，則雖有堯、舜之智，孟賁之勇，豈有不摧折者哉？

translations.literal: If he shakes them with awe and presses them with weight, then though there be the wisdom of Yao and Shun, the courage of Meng Ben, how could there be any who are not shattered and broken?
translations.idiomatic: Threaten them with terror and crush them with power, and not even a Yao or Shun in wisdom, a Meng Ben in courage, could avoid being broken.

sentences.zh: 如此，則人主不得聞其過失矣；

translations.literal: In this way the human lord does not get to hear his faults.
translations.idiomatic: Then the ruler never hears his own errors.

sentences.zh: 弗聞，則社稷危矣。

translations.literal: If he does not hear, then the altars of soil and grain are in danger.
translations.idiomatic: If he does not hear them, the altars are in peril.

sentences.zh: 古者聖王之制，史在前書過失，工誦箴諫，瞽誦詩諫，公卿比諫，士傳言諫，庶人謗於道，商旅議於市，然後君得聞其過失也。

translations.literal: In the regulations of the sage kings of antiquity, the scribe stood in front and wrote down faults, the artisan chanted admonitions, the blind chanted odes of remonstrance, the dukes and ministers offered successive remonstrance, scholars transmitted words of remonstrance, commoners slandered on the road, merchants debated in the market—only then did the lord get to hear his faults.
translations.idiomatic: The ancient sage kings set it so: the scribe recorded misdeeds before the throne, craftsmen sang warnings, blind musicians sang corrective odes, nobles remonstrated in turn, rumor reached the court from scholars, commoners could speak on the road, and merchants argue in the market—only thus did a ruler learn his faults.

sentences.zh: 聞其過失而改之，見義而從之，所以永有天下也。

translations.literal: Hearing his faults he corrected them, seeing what was right he followed it; by this he long possessed the empire.
translations.idiomatic: He amended what he heard was wrong and embraced what he saw was right; that is how dynasties endure.

sentences.zh: 天子之尊，四海之內，其義莫不為臣。

translations.literal: The honor of the Son of Heaven—within the four seas, in principle none is not his subject.
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven is supreme: within the four seas all owe him subjection in principle.

sentences.zh: 然而養三老於大學，親執醬而饋，執爵而□，祝饐在前，祝鯁在後，公卿奉杖，大夫進履，舉賢以自輔弼，求修正之士使直諫。

translations.literal: Yet he nourishes the three elders in the Great Academy, personally holds the sauce and feeds them, holds the goblet and [lacuna], the feeder of soft food stands in front, the feeder of hard food stands behind, dukes and ministers offer staffs, great officers advance shoes, raises the worthy to assist himself, seeks men of refinement and correction to cause straight remonstrance.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he would feed the three elders in the Imperial Academy with his own hands on the sauce and cup, with attendants chanting warnings against choking before him and against gagging behind him, while nobles offered staffs and shoes and he summoned worthies to counsel him and upright men to speak plainly.

sentences.zh: 故以天子之尊，尊養三老，視孝也；

translations.literal: Therefore, with the honor of the Son of Heaven, honoring and nourishing the three elders—this is showing filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: Thus even Heaven’s Son honors the three elders—that is filial piety made visible.

sentences.zh: 立輔弼之臣者，恐驕也；

translations.literal: Establishing ministers who assist and support—this is fearing arrogance.
translations.idiomatic: He sets up ministers to steady him—that is guarding against pride.

sentences.zh: 置直諫之士者，恐不得聞其過也；

translations.literal: Placing straight-remonstrance scholars—this is fearing not hearing his faults.
translations.idiomatic: He keeps plain-speaking advisers—that is fearing to miss his faults.

sentences.zh: 學問至於芻蕘者，求獸無饜也；

translations.literal: That inquiry and learning reach the firewood-gatherer—this is seeking good without satiety.
translations.idiomatic: That he would take counsel even from a wood-gatherer shows a ruler insatiable in seeking what is good.

sentences.zh: 商人庶人誹謗已而改之，從善無不聽也。

translations.literal: Merchants and commoners slandered him and he corrected it; following the good, there was none he did not listen to.
translations.idiomatic: When merchants or commoners criticized him, he changed course; every good word found a hearing.

sentences.zh: 昔者，秦政力並萬國，富有天下，破六國以為郡縣，築長城以為關塞。

translations.literal: In former times, Qin’s government by force annexed the myriad states, was rich as possessor of the empire, broke the six states to make commanderies and counties, built the Long Wall to serve as passes and barriers.
translations.idiomatic: Once Qin’s armies had swallowed the feudal states and owned the wealth of the realm, it carved the old kingdoms into commanderies and counties and ran the Long Wall as its outer bolt.

sentences.zh: 秦地之固，大小之勢，輕重之權，其與一家之富，一夫之強，胡可勝計也！

translations.literal: The solidity of Qin land, the configuration of great and small, the balance of light and heavy—how can these be reckoned against the wealth of one house or the strength of one man!
translations.idiomatic: Qin’s terrain, its scale of forces, its weight in the balance—how could that be compared to the fortune of one clan or the muscle of a single man?

sentences.zh: 然而兵破於陳涉，地奪於劉氏者，何也？

translations.literal: Yet its armies were broken by Chen She, its lands seized by the Liu clan—why?
translations.idiomatic: Yet Chen She broke its armies and the house of Liu stripped its territory—why?

sentences.zh: 秦王貪狼暴虐，殘賊天下，窮困萬民，以適其欲也。

translations.literal: The King of Qin was greedy as a wolf, violent and cruel, injuring and plundering the empire, impoverishing the myriad people, to suit his desires.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor was rapacious and cruel; he bled the realm and beggared the people to serve his appetites.

sentences.zh: 昔者，周蓋千八百國，以九州之民養千八百國之君，用民之力不過歲三日，什一而籍，君有餘財，民有餘力，而頌聲作。

translations.literal: In former times, Zhou in all likelihood had eighteen hundred states; with the people of the nine provinces it nourished the lords of eighteen hundred states; the use of the people’s strength did not exceed three days a year; one-tenth in tax and registration; the lord had surplus wealth, the people had surplus strength, and songs of praise arose.
translations.idiomatic: Under the Zhou order there were said to be some eighteen hundred states; the nine provinces fed eighteen hundred rulers, corvée rarely exceeded three days a year, and the tithe was the rule—so the treasury stayed full, the people strong, and praise was heard.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝以千八百國之民自養，力罷不能勝其役，財盡不能勝其求。

translations.literal: The Qin emperor used the people of eighteen hundred states to nourish himself alone; strength was exhausted and could not bear his labor, wealth was exhausted and could not bear his demands.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor made the people of those eighteen hundred polities feed him alone, until strength could not bear his levies nor wealth his demands.

sentences.zh: 一君之身耳，所以自養者馳騁弋獵之娛，天下弗能供也。

translations.literal: The body of one lord only—what he used to nourish himself was the pleasure of galloping and hunting; the empire could not supply it.
translations.idiomatic: One man’s body—and the hunting and racing he wanted could not be supplied by the whole empire.

sentences.zh: 勞罷者不得休息，饑寒者不得衣食，亡罪而死刑者無所告訴，人與之為怨，家與之為仇，故天下壞也。

translations.literal: Those worn by labor obtained no rest; those cold and hungry obtained no clothing or food; those without crime who faced death by execution had nowhere to appeal; men made resentment with him, households made enmity with him; therefore the empire was ruined.
translations.idiomatic: The overworked had no rest, the hungry no clothes or food, the innocent condemned had no appeal; every man nursed a grudge, every house a feud—so the realm collapsed.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝身在之時，天下已壞矣，而弗自知也。

translations.literal: While the Qin emperor was still in person, the empire was already ruined, yet he did not himself know it.
translations.idiomatic: The world was already coming apart while the First Emperor still lived, and he never saw it.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝東巡狩，至會稽、琅邪，刻石著其功，自以為過堯、舜統；

translations.literal: The Qin emperor toured east on inspection, reaching Kuaiji and Langya, carved stone to display his merit, and thought himself superior to Yao and Shun in succession.
translations.idiomatic: He toured the east as far as Kuaiji and Langya, cut inscriptions to boast his deeds, and imagined his line outshining Yao and Shun.

sentences.zh: 縣石鑄鐘□，篩土築阿房之宮，自以為萬世有天下也。

translations.literal: Suspended stones to cast bells [lacuna], sifted earth to build the palace of Epang, and thought himself to possess the empire for ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: He hung stone to cast bells and stands, piled earth for Epang, and believed his throne would last ten thousand generations.

sentences.zh: 古者聖王作謚，三四十世耳，雖堯、舜、禹、湯、文、武累世廣德以為子孫基業，無過二三十世者也。

translations.literal: In antiquity the sage kings instituted posthumous titles—only thirty or forty generations; though Yao, Shun, Yu, Tang, Wen, and Wu piled up generations of broad virtue to be the foundation for descendants, none exceeded twenty or thirty generations.
translations.idiomatic: The ancient sage kings used posthumous names, yet lines ran only thirty or forty generations; even Yao, Shun, Yu, Tang, Wen, and Wu, for all their piled virtue, handed down no more than twenty or thirty reigns.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝曰死而以謚法，是父子名號有時相襲也，以一至萬，則世世不相復也，故死而號曰始皇帝，其次曰二世皇帝者，欲以一至萬也。

translations.literal: The Qin emperor said: after death to use the posthumous-title method is for father and son’s names sometimes to succeed one another; from one to ten thousand, then generation after generation would not repeat; therefore on death he styled himself First Emperor, and the next was styled Second Emperor—he desired to go from one to ten thousand.
translations.idiomatic: He declared that posthumous titles would let father and son share a style in turn; counting from one to ten thousand, no reign-name need ever repeat—hence he called himself First Emperor and his heir Second Emperor, as if he could count his line to infinity.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝計其功德，度其後嗣，世世無窮，然身死才數月耳，天下四面而攻之，宗廟滅絕矣。

translations.literal: The Qin emperor reckoned his merit and virtue, measured his posterity—generation after generation without end—yet his body had been dead only several months when the empire attacked him from four sides and the ancestral temple was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: He measured his merit and planned an endless succession, yet within months of his death the realm rose on every side and his ancestral temple was extinguished.

sentences.zh: 秦皇帝居滅絕之中而不自知者何也？

translations.literal: Why was it that the Qin emperor dwelt in extinction and did not himself know?
translations.idiomatic: How could he sit in the midst of ruin and not know it?

sentences.zh: 天下莫敢告也。

translations.literal: In the empire none dared tell him.
translations.idiomatic: Because no one in the realm dared tell him.

sentences.zh: 其所以莫敢告者何也？

translations.literal: What was the reason none dared tell him?
translations.idiomatic: Why did no one dare?

sentences.zh: 亡養老之義，亡輔弼之臣，亡進諫之士，縱恣行誅，退誹謗之人，殺直諫之士，是以道諛偷合苟容，比其德則賢於堯、舜，課其功則賢於湯、武，天下已潰而莫之告也。

translations.literal: He lacked the righteousness of nourishing the aged; he lacked ministers who assist and support; he lacked scholars who advance remonstrance; he gave free rein to executions, dismissed those who slandered, killed those who remonstrated straight—therefore fawning men stole agreement and looked for accommodation; comparing his virtue, then he was worthier than Yao and Shun; reckoning his merit, then he was worthier than Tang and Wu; the empire was already in collapse and none told him.
translations.idiomatic: There was no duty to care for elders, no ministers to steady him, no path for frank advice; he killed critics and silenced slander while flatterers compared him to Yao and Shun and rated his deeds above Tang and Wu—so the realm rotted while no voice warned him.

sentences.zh: 詩曰：「匪言不能，胡此畏忌，聽言則對，譖言則退。」

translations.literal: The Odes say: "It is not that they cannot speak—why this fear and dread? They answer when they hear words, but withdraw when they hear slander."
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry says: "It is not that they cannot speak—but why this dread? They answer when spoken to fair, yet shrink from slander."

sentences.zh: 此之謂也。

translations.literal: This speaks of it.
translations.idiomatic: That is what is meant.

sentences.zh: 又曰：「濟濟多士，文王以寧。」

translations.literal: Again it says: "Many were the officers, and by them King Wen enjoyed tranquility."
translations.idiomatic: It also says: "In such numbers stood the officers, and King Wen had peace."

sentences.zh: 天下未嘗亡士也，然而文王獨言以寧者何也？

translations.literal: The empire has never lacked officers; yet why is it that King Wen alone is said to have enjoyed tranquility?
translations.idiomatic: The world has never lacked able men—so why does the ode single out King Wen’s peace?

sentences.zh: 文王好仁則仁興，得士而敬之則士用，用之有禮義。

translations.literal: King Wen loved benevolence and benevolence rose; he obtained officers and respected them and then officers were employed; there was employment with ritual and righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Because King Wen loved humaneness, humaneness flourished; because he won men of talent and treated them with respect, they served him—and he employed them with ritual and right.

sentences.zh: 故不致其愛敬，則不能盡其心；

translations.literal: Therefore if he does not extend his love and respect, he cannot exhaust their hearts.
translations.idiomatic: Without genuine love and respect he cannot win their hearts.

sentences.zh: 不能盡其心，則不能盡其力；

translations.literal: If he cannot exhaust their hearts, he cannot exhaust their strength.
translations.idiomatic: If he does not win their hearts, he will not have their full strength.

sentences.zh: 不能盡其力，則不能成其功。

translations.literal: If he cannot exhaust their strength, he cannot complete his merit.
translations.idiomatic: Without their full strength he cannot complete his great work.

sentences.zh: 故古之賢君於其臣也，尊其爵祿而親之；

translations.literal: Therefore the worthy lords of antiquity toward their ministers honored their ranks and stipends and drew them close.
translations.idiomatic: So the wise kings of old honored rank and salary and drew their ministers near.

sentences.zh: 疾則臨視之亡數，死則往吊哭之，臨其小斂大斂，已棺塗而後為之服錫衰麻絰，而三臨其喪；

translations.literal: When ill he repeatedly came in person to look on them; when dead he went to mourn and weep for them, attended their small and great dressing of the corpse, and only after the coffin was sealed and plastered did he wear fine hemp and hemp headband, and thrice attended their mourning.
translations.idiomatic: If a minister fell ill, the ruler visited again and again; if he died, the ruler mourned, attended the successive shroudings, waited until the coffin was sealed, then donned mourning and thrice came to the rites.

sentences.zh: 未斂不飲酒食肉，未葬不舉樂，當宗廟之祭而死，為之廢樂。

translations.literal: Before dressing he did not drink wine or eat meat; before burial he did not raise music; if he died during the sacrifice at the ancestral temple, for him they stopped the music.
translations.idiomatic: Until the corpse was laid out he took no wine or meat; until burial he ordered no music; if a death fell during an ancestral offering, the music ceased for him.

sentences.zh: 故古之君人者於其臣也，可謂盡禮矣；

translations.literal: Therefore the lords of men of antiquity toward their ministers can be called having exhausted ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Such was the ritual ancient rulers showed their ministers.

sentences.zh: 服法服，端容貌，正顏色。

translations.literal: He wore the regulated robes, corrected his appearance, straightened his complexion.
translations.idiomatic: He put on court dress, set his bearing, and composed his face.

sentences.zh: 然後見之。

translations.literal: Only then did he meet them.
translations.idiomatic: Only then did he receive them.

sentences.zh: 故臣下莫敢不竭力盡死以報其上，功德立於後世，而令聞不忘也。

translations.literal: Therefore among ministers and subordinates none dared not exhaust strength and give death in repaying their superiors; merit and virtue were established for later generations, and fair fame was not forgotten.
translations.idiomatic: Then no minister held back life or strength in repaying his lord, and their merit lived after them with an undying name.

sentences.zh: 今陛下念思祖考，術追厥功，圖所以昭光洪業休德，使天下舉賢良方正之士，天下皆訢訢焉，曰將興堯、舜之道，三王之功矣。

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty recalls and thinks on his ancestors, traces and pursues their merit, plans how to display and broaden the great enterprise and beautiful virtue, causing the empire to raise worthy and upright scholars; all under heaven are joyful, saying they will revive the way of Yao and Shun and the merit of the three kings.
translations.idiomatic: Today Your Majesty remembers the ancestors, follows their achievements, and seeks to magnify a glorious rule; you have called for worthy and upright men across the realm, and the people rejoice, saying the ways of Yao and Shun and the three kings will rise again.

sentences.zh: 天下之士莫不精白以承休德。

translations.literal: The scholars of the empire none do not purify themselves to receive the beautiful virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Scholars everywhere polish themselves to answer that summoning virtue.

sentences.zh: 今方正之士皆在朝廷矣，又選其賢者使為常侍諸吏，與之馳驅射獵，一日再三出。

translations.literal: Now the upright scholars are all at court; again he selects their worthies and makes them constant attendants and various clerks, and with them gallops, races, and hunts, going out three times in one day.
translations.idiomatic: Those upright men are already at court—yet Your Majesty picks the best among them for constant attendance and then rides and hunts with them, sometimes three sorties in a single day.

sentences.zh: 臣恐朝廷之解馳，百官之墮於事也，諸侯聞之，又必怠於政矣。

translations.literal: Your servant fears the court’s loosening, the hundred officials’ falling behind in their tasks; when the feudal lords hear of it, they again will be slack in government.
translations.idiomatic: I fear the court will grow lax, officials will neglect their duties, and when the regional lords hear of it they too will slacken in government.

sentences.zh: 古者大臣不媟，故君子不常見其齊嚴之色、肅敬之容。

translations.literal: In antiquity great ministers were not lewd; therefore the gentleman did not constantly see their severe and orderly color and respectful bearing.
translations.idiomatic: In old times high ministers were not treated lightly, so a gentleman did not daily see their grave mien and reverent bearing.

sentences.zh: 大臣不得與宴游，方正修潔之士不得從射獵，使皆務其方以高其節，則群臣莫敢不正身修行，盡心以稱大禮。

translations.literal: Great ministers may not join in feasts and roaming; upright and pure scholars may not follow in shooting and hunting—if all are made to attend to their proper roles and raise their integrity, then among the host of ministers none will dare not correct body and conduct and exhaust heart in matching the great rites.
translations.idiomatic: Keep great officers from idle feasting and hunts, and keep upright scholars from the chase; let each mind his station and lift his integrity, and the whole court will order person and conduct to match the great ritual.

sentences.zh: 如此，則陛下之道尊敬，功業施於四海，垂於萬世子孫矣。

translations.literal: In this way Your Majesty’s way will be honored in respect, merit and enterprise will extend to the four seas and hang for myriad generations of descendants.
translations.idiomatic: Then your way will be held in awe, your work will reach the four seas, and your descendants for ages will inherit it.

sentences.zh: 誠不如此，則行日壞而榮日滅矣。

translations.literal: If it is truly not so, then conduct daily deteriorates and glory daily vanishes.
translations.idiomatic: If not, conduct rots day by day and glory fades.

sentences.zh: 夫士修之於家，而壞之於天子之廷，臣竊愍之。

translations.literal: As for the scholar who cultivates himself in his household but is ruined in the Son of Heaven’s court, your servant privately pities him.
translations.idiomatic: It pains me to see a man refine himself at home only to be undone in the imperial hall.

sentences.zh: 陛下與眾臣宴游，與大臣方正朝廷論議。

translations.literal: Your Majesty should feast and roam with the host of ministers, and with great ministers who are upright discuss matters in court.
translations.idiomatic: Let Your Majesty sport with the many officials, but debate policy in court with your gravest ministers.

sentences.zh: 夫游不失樂，朝不失禮，議不失計，軌事之大者也。

translations.literal: Then roaming loses not joy, court loses not ritual, discussion loses not planning—this is the great norm of handling affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Then pleasure need not cost dignity, audience need not cost ritual, and counsel need not cost good planning—that is the right way to govern.

sentences.zh: 其後，文帝除鑄錢令，山復上書諫，以為變先帝法，非是。

translations.literal: Afterward, Emperor Wen removed the coin-casting order; Shan again submitted a memorial remonstrating, holding that changing the former emperors’ laws was not right.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Emperor Wen lifted the ban on private minting, Jia Shan again remonstrated, arguing that to alter the laws of the former emperors was wrong.

sentences.zh: 又訟淮南王無大罪，宜急令反國。

translations.literal: Again he argued that the King of Huainan had no great crime and should urgently be ordered to return to his state.
translations.idiomatic: He also urged that the King of Huainan had committed no grave offense and should be sent back to his kingdom at once.

sentences.zh: 又言柴唐子為不善，足以戒。

translations.literal: Again he said that Zhai Tangzi’s conduct was improper, sufficient for a warning.
translations.idiomatic: He also warned that Zhai Tangzi’s misconduct was enough to serve as a lesson.

sentences.zh: 章下詰責，對以為：「錢者，亡用器也，而可以易富貴。

translations.literal: The memorial was sent down for interrogation and reproach; he answered holding that: "Coin is a useless vessel, yet it can exchange for wealth and honor.
translations.idiomatic: The document was returned with questions; he replied that money is useless in itself yet buys wealth and rank.

sentences.zh: 富貴者，人主之操柄也，令民為之，是與人主共操柄，不可長也。」

translations.literal: Wealth and honor are what the human lord holds as his handle; if you order the people to make it, this is sharing the handle with the human lord—it cannot be long sustained."
translations.idiomatic: Wealth and rank are the ruler’s levers; letting the common people mint is to share those levers with the throne, and that cannot last."

sentences.zh: 其言多激切，善指事意，然終不加罰，所以廣諫爭之路也。

translations.literal: His words were mostly cutting and sharp, good at pointing to the matter’s intent, yet in the end he was not punished—by this the road of remonstrance and contention was broadened.
translations.idiomatic: His language was often blunt and pointed squarely at the issue, yet he was never punished—so the court kept the channels of debate open.

sentences.zh: 其後復禁鑄錢雲。

translations.literal: Afterward again coin casting was forbidden, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Minting was later forbidden again, or so the record runs.

sentences.zh: 鄒陽，齊人也。

translations.literal: Zou Yang was a man of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Zou Yang was a native of Qi.

sentences.zh: 漢興，諸侯王皆自治民聘賢。

translations.literal: When Han rose, the feudal kings all governed their people and hired the worthy.
translations.idiomatic: When the Han arose, each feudal king ruled his own people and recruited worthy men.

sentences.zh: 吳王濞招致四方游士，陽與吳嚴忌、枚乘等俱仕吳，皆以文辯著名。

translations.literal: The King of Wu Liu Pi summoned wandering scholars from the four quarters; Yang together with Wu’s Yan Ji, Mei Sheng, and others all served Wu, and all were famous for literary disputation.
translations.idiomatic: King Liu Pi of Wu gathered wandering literati; Zou Yang served him alongside Yan Ji of Wu, Mei Sheng, and others, and they all won fame for their eloquence and letters.

sentences.zh: 久之，吳王以太子事怨望，稱疾不朝，陰有邪謀，陽奏書諫。

translations.literal: After a long time, the King of Wu, on account of the heir-apparent affair, harbored resentment, claimed illness and did not attend court, secretly had wicked plots, openly submitted memorials remonstrating.
translations.idiomatic: In time the King of Wu, nursing a grudge over the crown-prince affair, feigned illness and stayed away from court while plotting in secret and, on the surface, lodging memorials of remonstrance.

sentences.zh: 為其事尚隱，惡指斥言，故先引秦為諭，因道胡、越、齊、趙、淮南之難，然後乃致其意。

translations.literal: Because the matter was still hidden, he hated to point and name it; therefore he first cited Qin as a parable, then spoke of the troubles of Hu, Yue, Qi, Zhao, and Huainan, and only afterward brought out his meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Because the matter was still veiled, he would not name it outright; he opened with Qin as his mirror, then rehearsed the crises of the Hu, Yue, Qi, Zhao, and Huainan regions before he would speak his mind.

sentences.zh: 其辭曰：

translations.literal: Its text says:
translations.idiomatic: The memorial runs:

sentences.zh: 臣聞秦倚曲台之官，懸衡天下，畫地而不犯，兵加胡、越；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Qin leaned on the offices of the Curved Terrace, hung the balance on the empire, drew lines on the ground and none violated them, and applied arms to Hu and Yue.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard how Qin trusted its Curved Terrace bureaucracy, weighed the whole realm in its scales, drew its boundaries so that none might cross, and sent armies against the Hu and the Yue.

sentences.zh: 至其晚節末路，張耳、陳勝連從兵之據，以叩函谷，咸陽遂危。

translations.literal: When it came to its late years and final road, Zhang Er and Chen Sheng linked the bases of joined armies, to knock at Hangu Pass, and Xianyang was then in peril.
translations.idiomatic: Yet in its last days Zhang Er and Chen Sheng chained rebel bases together, beat on the Hangu barrier, and Xianyang itself trembled.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 列郡不相親，萬室不相救也。

translations.literal: The commanderies were not close to one another, the myriad households did not rescue one another.
translations.idiomatic: Because the commanderies did not stand together and the people could not rescue one another.

sentences.zh: 今胡數涉北河之外，上覆飛鳥，下不見伏菟，斗城不休，救兵不止，死者相隨，輦車相屬，轉粟流輸，千里不絕。

translations.literal: Now the Hu repeatedly ford north of the Northern River; above they cover the flying birds, below one does not see the crouching hare; the war of ramparts does not cease, relief troops do not stop; the dead follow one another, supply carts link in succession, turning grain and flowing transport, a thousand li without break.
translations.idiomatic: Today the Hu raid again and again beyond the northern He; their dust hides the birds in the sky and the hare on the ground; siege works never rest nor do relief columns; the dead fall in windrows, grain carts stretch in an endless chain for a thousand li.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 強趙責於河間，六齊望於惠後，城陽顧於盧博，三淮南之心思墳墓。

translations.literal: Strong Zhao presses at Hejian, the six Qi look to the Empress Hui, Chengyang looks toward Lu and Bo, the hearts of the three Huainan princes turn to their ancestral graves.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao presses hard at Hejian, the Qi domains watch the house of Empress Hui, Chengyang casts its eye toward Lu and Bo, and the three Huainan lines ache for their old tombs.

sentences.zh: 大王不憂，臣恐救兵之不專，胡馬遂進窺於邯鄲，越水長沙，還舟青陽。

translations.literal: If the great king is not worried, your servant fears the relief troops will not be of one mind, Hu horses will then advance to spy on Handan, Yue boats will reach Changsha, and returning ships will reach Qingyang.
translations.idiomatic: If Your Highness does not take alarm, I fear the allies will never act as one: Hu cavalry will press on Handan, Yue hulls will thread Changsha, and their fleets double back toward Qingyang.

sentences.zh: 雖使梁並淮陽之兵，下淮東，越廣陵，以遏越人之糧，漢亦折西河而下，北守漳水，以輔大國，胡亦益進，越亦益深。

translations.literal: Even if you made Liang combine Huaiyang's troops, descend east of the Huai, cross Guangling to cut the Yue people's grain, Han would also fold west of the He and descend, guard north at the Zhang River to assist the great state, Hu would also advance the more, Yue would also go the deeper.
translations.idiomatic: Even were Liang to join Huaiyang's hosts, sweep the Huai east, and cross Guangling to choke the Yue grain lines, the court could still wheel west of the great bend, hold the line on the Zhang, and stiffen the great kingdom—while the Hu push deeper and the Yue drive farther in.

sentences.zh: 此臣之所以大王患也。

translations.literal: This is what your servant therefore takes as the great king's worry.
translations.idiomatic: That, my lord, is the peril I see hanging over you.

sentences.zh: 臣聞交龍襄首奮翼，則浮雲出流，霧雨咸集。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that when paired dragons coil heads and flap wings, then floating clouds emerge in streams and fog and rain all gather.
translations.idiomatic: They say when twin dragons rear and beat their wings, clouds stream out and rain gathers.

sentences.zh: 聖王底節修德，則游談之士歸義思名。

translations.literal: When a sage king curbs his pace and cultivates virtue, then wandering talkers return to righteousness and long for fame.
translations.idiomatic: When a sage king reins himself in and builds virtue, wandering scholars flock to his justice and hunger for a name.

sentences.zh: 今臣盡智畢議，易精極慮，則無國不可奸；

translations.literal: Now if your servant exhausts wisdom and completes counsel, exchanges essence and exhausts thought, then no state cannot be violated.
translations.idiomatic: Were I to pour out every plan and polish every scheme, there is no kingdom I could not undermine.

sentences.zh: 飾固陋之心，則何王之門不可曳長裾乎？

translations.literal: If I adorn a narrow and base heart, then what king's gate could I not trail a long robe through?
translations.idiomatic: Were I to flatter a petty heart, there is no ruler's gate where I could not drag a long scholar's hem.

sentences.zh: 然臣所以歷數王之朝，背淮千里而自致者，非惡臣國而樂吳民也，竊高下風之行，尤說大王之義。

translations.literal: Yet the reason your servant has crossed the courts of many kings, turned his back on the Huai a thousand li and come of himself, is not that he hates his state and delights in Wu's people; he secretly admires the conduct of high and low wind, and especially delights in the great king's righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Yet I have crossed many courts and left the Huai a thousand li behind not because I despise my home and love Wu's folk, but because I honor your reputation and am drawn above all to your sense of right.

sentences.zh: 故願大王之無忽，察聽其志。

translations.literal: Therefore I hope the great king will not neglect it, examine and listen to his intent.
translations.idiomatic: So I beg you not to brush this aside: read my meaning and hear me out.

sentences.zh: 臣聞鷙鳥累百，不如一鶚。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that a hundred fierce birds are not equal to one osprey.
translations.idiomatic: A hundred common raptors do not match one eagle.

sentences.zh: 夫全趙之時，武力鼎士衣玄服叢台之下者一旦成市，而不能止幽王之湛患。

translations.literal: When Zhao was whole, armored warriors and tripod men in black clothes below the Cong Terrace became a market in one morning, yet they could not stop King You of Zhao's deep calamity.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao still stood intact, armored bravos thronged the Cong Terrace like a market in a single dawn, yet they could not spare King You of Zhao his doom.

sentences.zh: 淮南連山東之俠，死士盈朝，不能還厲王之西也。

translations.literal: Huainan linked the knights east of the mountains, dead soldiers filled the court, yet could not turn back King Li of Huainan's march west.
translations.idiomatic: Huainan rallied the swordsmen east of the mountains and filled its halls with men sworn to die, yet it could not stop King Li's drive westward.

sentences.zh: 然而計議不得，雖諸、賁不能安其位，亦明矣。

translations.literal: Yet if plans miss the mark, though there be Zhu and Ben they cannot secure their positions—this too is clear.
translations.idiomatic: When strategy fails, not even warriors like Zhu Hai and Meng Ben can hold their ground—that much is plain.

sentences.zh: 故願大王審畫而已。

translations.literal: Therefore I hope the great king will examine the plan and stop.
translations.idiomatic: I urge you, my lord, to weigh this counsel and stay your hand.

sentences.zh: 始孝文皇帝據關入立，寒心銷志，不明求衣。

translations.literal: When at first Emperor Xiaowen took the passes and entered to be established, his heart was chilled and his will melted; before dawn he sought his robes.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Wen first came through the passes to take the throne, he woke in dread before dawn and groped for his robes.

sentences.zh: 自立天子之後，使東牟硃虛東褒義父之後，深割嬰兒王之。

translations.literal: After he established himself as Son of Heaven, he sent the Marquis of Dongmou and the Marquis of Zhuxu east to ennoble the posterity of Emperor Yi the Righteous, deeply carved away territory, and enthroned infants as kings.
translations.idiomatic: Once enthroned, he sent the lords of Dongmou and Zhuxu east to honor the house of Emperor Yi of Chu, carved away great domains, and set infants on those thrones.

sentences.zh: 壤子王梁、代，益以淮陽。

translations.literal: He made neighboring sons kings of Liang and Dai, and added Huaiyang.
translations.idiomatic: He enfeoffed younger sons on Liang and Dai and enlarged their domains with Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 卒僕濟北，囚弟於雍者，豈非象新垣平等哉！

translations.literal: In the end he crushed the Prince of Jibei, imprisoned his younger brother at Yong—was this not patterned on Xinyuan Ping and the like!
translations.idiomatic: In the end he destroyed the Prince of Jibei and caged his own brother at Yong—was that not the very trick of men like Xinyuan Ping?

sentences.zh: 今天子新據先帝之遺業，左規山東，右制關中，變權易勢，大臣難知。

translations.literal: Now the Son of Heaven has newly taken the former emperor's legacy, rules Shandong with his left hand and the Guanzhong heartland with his right, shifts power and alters weight; great ministers are hard to read.
translations.idiomatic: The present emperor has just inherited the old throne: he grips the east with one hand and the interior with the other, shifting leverage at will, and no one can tell what his high ministers intend.

sentences.zh: 大王弗察，臣恐周鼎復起於漢，新垣過計於朝，則我吳遺嗣，不可期於世矣。

translations.literal: If the great king does not examine it, your servant fears the Zhou tripod will again rise in Han, Xinyuan's excess of plans in court, then our Wu's surviving heirs cannot be hoped for in the generation.
translations.idiomatic: If you do not see this clearly, I fear the royal cauldron will pass to Han once more, another Xinyuan will overreach in court, and the house of Wu will have no heirs to count on.

sentences.zh: 高皇帝燒棧道，水章邯，兵不留行，收弊民之倦，東馳函谷，西楚大破。

translations.literal: The High Emperor burned the plank roads, attacked Zhang Han by water, his army did not linger in marching, gathered the weariness of a worn people, galloped east to Hangu, and Western Chu was greatly broken.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu burned the mountain roads, drowned Zhang Han's armies, never let his columns slow, rallied a spent people, raced east to Hangu, and shattered Western Chu.

sentences.zh: 水攻則章邯以亡其城，陸擊則荊王以失其地，此皆國家之不幾者也。

translations.literal: By water attack then Zhang Han thereby lost his cities; by land strike then the King of Jing thereby lost his lands—these are all cases where the state had no margin.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Han lost his cities to the flood, the King of Jing his lands to the blade—both were powers with no margin left.

sentences.zh: 願大王孰察之。

translations.literal: I hope the great king examines it carefully.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you, my lord, to weigh this with care.

sentences.zh: 吳王不內其言。

translations.literal: The King of Wu did not accept his words.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Wu would not heed him.

sentences.zh: 是時，景帝少弟梁孝王貴盛，亦待士。

translations.literal: At this time Emperor Jing's younger brother King Xiao of Liang was noble and flourishing, and likewise entertained scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Emperor Jing's younger brother, King Xiao of Liang, stood at the height of power and kept scholars about him.

sentences.zh: 於是鄒陽、枚乘、嚴忌知吳不可說，皆去之梁，從孝王游。

translations.literal: Thereupon Zou Yang, Mei Sheng, and Yan Ji, knowing Wu could not be persuaded, all left for Liang and roamed with King Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Zou Yang, Mei Sheng, and Yan Ji saw Wu was past persuasion and left for Liang to join King Xiao's circle.

sentences.zh: 陽為人有智略，慷慨不苟合，介於羊勝、公孫詭之間。

translations.literal: Yang as a man had wisdom and strategy, was high-spirited and did not lightly conform, and stood between Yang Sheng and Gongsun Gui.
translations.idiomatic: Zou Yang was clever and bold, slow to compromise, and moved between Yang Sheng and Gongsun Gui.

sentences.zh: 勝等疾陽，惡之孝王。

translations.literal: Sheng and the others hated Yang and slandered him to King Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Sheng and his clique envied him and whispered slander to King Xiao.

sentences.zh: 孝王怒，下陽吏，將殺之。

translations.literal: King Xiao was angry, handed Yang down to the clerks, and was about to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: The king flew into a rage, threw Zou Yang to the jailers, and prepared to execute him.

sentences.zh: 陽客遊以讒見禽，恐死而負累，乃從獄中上書曰：

translations.literal: Yang, a wandering guest, was seized on seeing slander; fearing death and bearing accumulated charges, from prison he submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: A wandering client seized on a whisper of malice, he faced death and a ruined name; from his cell he sent up this memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞忠無不報，信不見疑，臣常以為然，徒虛語耳。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that loyalty is never without reward, trust is never met with suspicion; your servant always held this true—mere empty words only.
translations.idiomatic: They say loyalty always meets its reward and good faith never meets doubt—I used to believe it; I see now it was only a saying.

sentences.zh: 昔荊軻慕燕丹之義，白虹貫日，太子畏之；

translations.literal: In olden times Jing Ke admired the righteousness of Prince Dan of Yan, a white rainbow pierced the sun, and the heir was afraid.
translations.idiomatic: When Jing Ke took up Prince Dan's cause, a white rainbow spanned the sun—yet the prince flinched.

sentences.zh: 衛先生為秦畫長平之事，太白食昂，昭王疑之。

translations.literal: Master Wei planned the Changping affair for Qin, the Great White devoured Mao, and King Zhao suspected him.
translations.idiomatic: When Master Wei laid the Changping plan for Qin, Venus swallowed Mao—and King Zhao turned against him.

sentences.zh: 夫精變天地而信不諭兩主，豈不哀哉！

translations.literal: Their essences moved Heaven and earth yet trust was not understood by the two lords—is it not lamentable!
translations.idiomatic: Their sincerity shook heaven and earth, yet neither ruler could trust them—how bitter that is.

sentences.zh: 今臣盡忠竭誠，畢議願知，左右不明，卒從吏訊，為世所疑。

translations.literal: Now your servant exhausts loyalty and pours out sincerity, completes counsel and hopes to be understood; those at left and right are not clear, in the end he follows the clerks' interrogation, and is doubted by the generation.
translations.idiomatic: I have given you my whole loyalty and every honest plan, yet those about you cannot see it; I end in the jailers' questions and the world calls me faithless.

sentences.zh: 是使荊軻、衛先生復起，而燕、秦不寤也。

translations.literal: This is to make Jing Ke and Master Wei rise again while Yan and Qin do not awake.
translations.idiomatic: You would raise Jing Ke and Master Wei from the grave while Yan and Qin still slept on.

sentences.zh: 原大王孰察之。

translations.literal: I hope the great king examines it carefully.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you, my lord, to look into this.

sentences.zh: 昔玉人獻寶，楚王誅之；

translations.literal: In olden times the jade worker presented a treasure and the King of Chu executed him.
translations.idiomatic: The jade carver once offered a gem and the King of Chu had him killed.

sentences.zh: 李斯竭忠，胡亥極刑。

translations.literal: Li Si exhausted loyalty and Huhai applied extreme punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Li Si gave his utmost loyalty and received the cruelest death from Huhai.

sentences.zh: 是以箕子陽狂，接輿避世，恐遭此患也。

translations.literal: Therefore Jizi feigned madness, Jieyu avoided the world, fearing to encounter this calamity.
translations.idiomatic: So Jizi played the madman and Jieyu fled the world, dreading that very fate.

sentences.zh: 願大王察玉人、李斯之意，而後楚王、胡亥之聽，毋使臣為箕子、接輿所笑。

translations.literal: I hope the great king will examine the intent of the jade worker and Li Si, and put behind the listening of the King of Chu and Huhai, and do not cause your servant to be laughed at by Jizi and Jieyu.
translations.idiomatic: Study the jade carver's and Li Si's hearts, turn away from the ears of the King of Chu and of Huhai, and do not make me a laughingstock to Jizi and Jieyu.

sentences.zh: 臣聞比干剖心，子胥鴟夷，臣始不信，乃今知之。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Bigan had his heart carved out, Zixu ended in a leather sack—your servant at first did not believe it; now he knows it.
translations.idiomatic: I had heard of Bigan's heart cut out and Wu Zixu sewn in a leather bag—I did not believe it; now I do.

sentences.zh: 願大王孰察，少加憐焉！

translations.literal: I hope the great king examines it carefully and slightly adds pity.
translations.idiomatic: Look closely, my lord, and show a little mercy.

sentences.zh: 語曰：「有白頭如新，傾蓋如故。」

translations.literal: A saying runs: "Some grow white-haired as strangers; some meet under a tilted canopy as old friends."
translations.idiomatic: The proverb says some remain strangers though their hair turns white, while others are old friends from the moment their canopies touch.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 知與不知也。

translations.literal: Knowing and not knowing.
translations.idiomatic: Because one was known and the other was not.

sentences.zh: 故樊於期逃秦之燕，借荊軻首以奉丹事；

translations.literal: Therefore Fan Wuji fled Qin to Yan, borrowed Jing Ke's head to serve Dan's affair.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Wuji fled Qin for Yan and lent Jing Ke his head to serve Prince Dan.

sentences.zh: 王奢去齊之魏，臨城自剄以卻齊而存魏。

translations.literal: Wang She left Qi for Wei, at the city wall cut his own throat to repel Qi and preserve Wei.
translations.idiomatic: Wang She left Qi for Wei and opened his own throat beneath the wall to drive Qi back and save Wei.

sentences.zh: 夫王奢、樊於期非新於齊、秦而故於燕、魏也，所以去二國死兩君者，行合於志，慕義無窮也。

translations.literal: As for Wang She and Fan Wuji, they were not fresh to Qi and Qin yet old to Yan and Wei; the reason they left the two states and died for the two lords was that conduct matched their will and they admired righteousness without end.
translations.idiomatic: Wang She and Fan Wuji were not newcomers to Qi and Qin nor strangers to Yan and Wei; they left two kingdoms and died for two lords because each man's deed matched his purpose and his love of right knew no limit.

sentences.zh: 是以蘇秦不信於天下，為燕尾生；

translations.literal: Therefore Su Qin was not trusted in the empire, yet for Yan he was Wei Sheng.
translations.idiomatic: Su Qin was distrusted everywhere, yet for Yan he kept faith like Wei Sheng.

sentences.zh: 自圭戰亡六城，為魏取中山。

translations.literal: Bai Gui in battle lost six cities, yet for Wei he took Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: Bai Gui lost six cities in war, then won Zhongshan back for Wei.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 誠有以相知也。

translations.literal: Truly there was means for mutual understanding.
translations.idiomatic: Because true understanding ran between them.

sentences.zh: 蘇秦相燕，人惡之燕王，燕王按劍而怒，食以□騠；

translations.literal: Su Qin was chancellor of Yan; men slandered him to the King of Yan; the King of Yan grasped his sword in anger and fed him with [lacuna] steeds.
translations.idiomatic: When Su Qin served Yan as chancellor, men slandered him; the king gripped his sword in fury—then feasted him on the finest horses.

sentences.zh: 白圭顯於中山，人惡之於魏文侯，文侯賜以夜光之璧。

translations.literal: Bai Gui was prominent in Zhongshan; men slandered him to Marquis Wen of Wei; Marquis Wen gave him the night-shining jade disk.
translations.idiomatic: When Bai Gui rose in Zhongshan, men maligned him to Marquis Wen of Wei, who then bestowed the luminous jade.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 兩主二臣，剖心析肝相信，豈移於浮辭哉！

translations.literal: The two lords and two ministers cut open hearts and split livers in mutual trust—how could they be shifted by floating words!
translations.idiomatic: Those two rulers and two ministers trusted each other to the marrow—no idle rumor could move them.

sentences.zh: 故女無美惡，入官見妒；

translations.literal: Therefore as for women, without beauty or ugliness, entering the palace they meet jealousy.
translations.idiomatic: In the harem beauty hardly matters: every woman meets envy.

sentences.zh: 士無賢不肖，入朝見嫉。

translations.literal: As for scholars, without worth or lack of worth, entering court they meet jealousy.
translations.idiomatic: At court talent hardly matters: every man meets envy.

sentences.zh: 昔司馬喜臏腳於宋，卒相中山；

translations.literal: In olden times Sima Xi had his feet cut off in Song, in the end he became chancellor of Zhongshan.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Xi was maimed in Song yet ended as minister of Zhongshan.

sentences.zh: 范睢拉脅折齒於魏，卒為應侯。

translations.literal: Fan Ju had ribs pulled and teeth broken in Wei, in the end became Marquis of Ying.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Ju had his ribs broken and his teeth smashed in Wei, yet rose to be Lord Ying.

sentences.zh: 此二人者，皆信必然之畫，捐朋黨之私，挾孤獨之交，故不能自免於嫉妒之人也。

translations.literal: These two men both trusted in plans that were sure to succeed, cast aside the selfishness of faction, and embraced solitary friendship; therefore they could not save themselves from jealous men.
translations.idiomatic: Both staked everything on a sure design, renounced clique interest, and stood alone with their lords—yet neither escaped the envious.

sentences.zh: 是以申徒狄蹈雍之河，徐衍負石入海。

translations.literal: Therefore Shentu Di trod the Yong River, Xu Yan shouldered a stone and entered the sea.
translations.idiomatic: So Shentu Di walked into the Yong and Xu Yan walked into the sea with a stone on his back.

sentences.zh: 不容於世，義不苟取比周於朝以移主上之心。

translations.literal: Not tolerated by the generation, in righteousness they would not lightly take faction in court to shift the mind of the lord above.
translations.idiomatic: They could not bear the age, yet they would not court a cabal at court to sway their ruler.

sentences.zh: 故百里奚乞食於道路，繆公委之以政；

translations.literal: Therefore Baili Xi begged food on the road, and Duke Mu of Qin entrusted him with government.
translations.idiomatic: Baili Xi begged on the road, yet Duke Mu of Qin handed him the government.

sentences.zh: 甯戚飯牛車下，桓公任之以國。

translations.literal: Ning Qi fed his ox beneath the cart, and Duke Huan of Qi employed him for the state.
translations.idiomatic: Ning Qi tended his ox under the wagon, yet Duke Huan of Qi made him steward of the realm.

sentences.zh: 此二人者，豈素宦於朝，借譽於左右，然後二主用之哉？

translations.literal: These two men—how could they have been long officials at court, borrowed reputation from those at left and right, and only then the two lords employed them?
translations.idiomatic: Did either owe his place to long service at court or praise from favorites?

sentences.zh: 感於心，合於行，堅如膠□，昆弟不能離，豈惑於眾口哉？

translations.literal: Moved in heart, joined in conduct, hard as glue and [lacuna], elder and younger brothers could not separate them—how could they be deluded by the mouths of the multitude?
translations.idiomatic: Heart answered to heart, deed to deed, bonded like glue—brothers could not have pulled them apart, let alone the crowd's tongue.

sentences.zh: 故偏聽生奸，獨任成亂。

translations.literal: Therefore partial listening breeds treachery, sole trust breeds chaos.
translations.idiomatic: A ruler who hears only one side invites treason; one who trusts a single voice courts chaos.

sentences.zh: 昔魯聽季孫之說逐孔子，宋任子冉之計囚墨翟。

translations.literal: In olden times Lu listened to Jisun's persuasion and expelled Confucius; Song employed Ziran's plan and imprisoned Mozi.
translations.idiomatic: Lu heeded Jisun and drove out Confucius; Song followed Ziran's counsel and caged Mozi.

sentences.zh: 夫以孔、墨之辯，不能自免於讒諛，而二國以危。

translations.literal: With the disputation of Confucius and Mozi they could not save themselves from slander, and the two states thereby were in peril.
translations.idiomatic: Men as eloquent as Confucius and Mozi could not save themselves from spite, and both states stumbled.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 眾口鑠金，積毀銷骨也。

translations.literal: The mouths of the multitude melt metal; piled slander melts bone.
translations.idiomatic: Because many tongues can melt bronze, and piled slander can wear away bone.

sentences.zh: 秦用戎人由余而伯中國，齊用越人子臧而強威、宣。

translations.literal: Qin employed the Rong man You Yu and became hegemon in the Central States; Qi employed the Yue man Zicang and strengthened itself under Kings Wei and Xuan.
translations.idiomatic: Qin used the Rong adviser You Yu and dominated the heartland; Qi used the Yue man Zicang and grew mighty under Wei and Xuan.

sentences.zh: 此二國豈繫於俗，牽於世，系奇偏之浮辭哉？

translations.literal: Could these two states have been tied by custom, dragged by the age, bound by odd partial floating words?
translations.idiomatic: Were those kingdoms slaves to fashion, rumor, or prejudice?

sentences.zh: 公聽並觀，垂明當世。

translations.literal: They listened openly and looked on all sides, letting brightness hang over the generation.
translations.idiomatic: They listened widely, looked everywhere, and let clear sight rest on the age.

sentences.zh: 故意合則胡、越為兄弟，由余、子臧是矣；

translations.literal: Therefore when intent matches, Hu and Yue become brothers—You Yu and Zicang are cases.
translations.idiomatic: When minds meet, Hu and Yue are brothers—that was You Yu and Zicang.

sentences.zh: 不合則骨肉為仇敵，硃、象、管、蔡是矣。

translations.literal: When intent does not match, flesh and bone become enemies—Danzhu, Xiang, Guan, and Cai are cases.
translations.idiomatic: When they do not, kin become foes—look at Danzhu and Xiang, or Guan and Cai of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 今人主誠能用齊、秦之明，後宋、魯之聽，則五伯不足侔，而三王易為也。

translations.literal: Now if the human lord truly can employ the clarity of Qi and Qin and put behind the listening of Song and Lu, then the Five Hegemons are not worth matching and the Three Kings are easy to do.
translations.idiomatic: Were a ruler to match Qi and Qin's breadth of hearing and shun Song and Lu's narrowness, he would outshine the Five Hegemons and rival the Three Kings with ease.

sentences.zh: 是以聖王覺寤，捐子之之心，而不說田常之賢，封比干之後，修孕婦之墓，故功業覆於天下。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage king awakens, casts aside the heart of Zizhi, and does not delight in Tian Chang's worthiness, enfeoffs the descendants of Bigan, repairs the tomb of the wronged pregnant woman—therefore merit and enterprise cover the empire.
translations.idiomatic: A waking sage casts off a Zizhi's flattery, refuses to dote on a Tian Chang's seeming virtue, ennobles Bigan's line, tends the tomb of the woman who died with her unborn child—such a ruler's fame covers the realm.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 欲善亡厭也。

translations.literal: Desiring good, there is no satiety.
translations.idiomatic: Because the hunger for good knows no end.

sentences.zh: 夫晉文親其仇，強伯諸侯；

translations.literal: As for Duke Wen of Jin, he drew close to his enemy and became strong hegemon over the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Wen of Jin embraced his old foe and rose to lord the nobles.

sentences.zh: 齊桓用其仇，而一匡天下。

translations.literal: Duke Huan of Qi employed his enemy and thereby unified correction under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi raised up his enemy and set the realm to rights.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 慈仁殷勤，誠加於心，不可以虛辭借也。

translations.literal: Kindness and benevolence diligent and earnest, sincerity applied to the heart—this cannot be borrowed with empty words.
translations.idiomatic: Because deep kindness and steady truth in the heart cannot be faked with fine phrases.

sentences.zh: 至夫秦用商鞅之法，東弱韓、魏，立強天下，卒車裂之。

translations.literal: As for how Qin employed the laws of Shang Yang, east it weakened Han and Wei, stood strong over the empire, in the end he was torn apart by chariots.
translations.idiomatic: Qin used Shang Yang's code to crush Han and Wei in the east and tower over the world—yet Shang Yang died under the cart wheels.

sentences.zh: 越用大夫種之謀，禽勁吳而伯中國，逆誅其身。

translations.literal: Yue employed Grand Officer Zhong's plan, captured mighty Wu and became hegemon in the Central States, yet contrary to principle his body was executed.
translations.idiomatic: Yue followed Zhong's counsel, broke mighty Wu, and ruled the heartland—yet Zhong himself was put to death.

sentences.zh: 是以孫叔敖三去相而不悔，於陵子仲辭三公為人灌園。

translations.literal: Therefore Sunshu Ao thrice left the chancellorship and did not regret it; Ziling Zizhong declined the three dukes' posts to water gardens for others.
translations.idiomatic: Sunshu Ao resigned the prime ministership three times without regret; Ziling Zizhong refused the three highest offices to hoe another man's garden.

sentences.zh: 今人主誠能去驕傲之心，懷可報之意，披心腹，見情素，墮肝膽，施德厚，終與之窮達，無愛於士，則桀之犬可使吠堯，跖之客可使刺由，何況因萬乘之權，假聖王之資乎！

translations.literal: Now if the human lord truly can cast aside the heart of pride, harbor intent that can be repaid, spread heart and belly, reveal plain feeling, drop liver and gall, extend virtue thickly, follow them to the end in poverty and success, have no stinting toward scholars, then Jie's dogs can be made to bark at Yao, Zhi's retainers can be made to stab You—how much the more when one rides the authority of ten thousand chariots and borrows the resources of a sage king!
translations.idiomatic: Were a ruler to shed arrogance, show himself worthy of devotion, open his breast, lay bare his good faith, give his whole substance, heap kindness on his men, and stand by them in want or wealth without stinting talent—even Jie's hounds could be set on Yao, Zhi's henchmen sent against You; how much truer when he wields the power of ten thousand chariots and wields a sage king's legacy!

sentences.zh: 然則荊軻湛七族，要離燔妻子，豈足為大王道哉！

translations.literal: If so, then Jing Ke's seven clans were drowned in punishment, Yaoliao burned wife and children—how can these suffice to be spoken to the great king!
translations.idiomatic: Would you have me rehearse how Jing Ke's kin were wiped out to the seventh degree, or how Yaoliao burned his wife and child—examples hardly fit for your ears!

sentences.zh: 臣聞明月之珠，夜光之璧，以暗投人於道，眾莫不按劍相眄者。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that bright moon pearls and night-shining jade disks, if in darkness one casts them to people on the road, the multitude none do not grasp sword and stare sidelong.
translations.idiomatic: They say that if you toss a famous pearl or a glowing jade to strangers in the dark, every passerby will reach for his sword and eye you with mistrust.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 無因而至前也。

translations.literal: Because there was no cause for them to arrive before.
translations.idiomatic: Because no one had vouched for you first.

sentences.zh: 蟠木根柢，輪囷離奇，而為萬乘器者，以左右先為之容也。

translations.literal: Twisted wood with root base, coiled and extraordinary, yet becomes a vessel for ten thousand chariots—because those at left and right first made appearance for it.
translations.idiomatic: A knotted stump may become the emperor's chariot timber only after his attendants have praised its grain.

sentences.zh: 故無因而至前，雖出隨珠和璧，祗怨結而不見德；

translations.literal: Therefore without cause arriving before, though one produce Sui pearls and He disks, only resentment is tied and virtue is not seen.
translations.idiomatic: Present such gifts unintroduced and you bind resentment, not gratitude.

sentences.zh: 有人先游，則枯木朽株，樹功而不忘。

translations.literal: If someone first introduces them, then withered wood and rotten stumps establish merit and are not forgotten.
translations.idiomatic: With a sponsor's word even deadwood wins lasting credit.

sentences.zh: 今夫天下布衣窮居之士，身在貧羸，雖蒙堯、舜之術、挾伊、管之辯，懷龍逢、比干之意，而素無根柢之容，雖竭精神，欲開忠於當世之君，則人主必龔按劍相眄之跡矣。

translations.literal: Now as for scholars of the empire in plain cloth dwelling in poverty, their persons in poverty and emaciation, though they cover themselves with Yao and Shun's arts, clasp Yi Yin and Guan Zhong's disputation, embrace Longpeng and Bigan's intent, yet plain without root-base appearance, though they exhaust spirit, desire to open loyalty to the ruler of the age, then the human lord surely must grasp sword and stare sidelong traces.
translations.idiomatic: Consider the poor scholar in homespun: he may wield the learning of Yao and Shun, the rhetoric of Yi Yin and Guan Zhong, the steadfast heart of Longpeng and Bigan—yet without patrons at court to polish his name, he can pour out his soul in loyal counsel and still meet only a ruler's hand on his hilt and a sidelong glare.

sentences.zh: 是使布衣之士不得為枯木朽株之資也。

translations.literal: This is to make scholars in plain cloth unable to obtain the means of withered wood and rotten stumps.
translations.idiomatic: Thus the man in homespun lacks even the advocate a rotten stump enjoys.

sentences.zh: 是以聖王制世御俗，獨化於陶鈞之上，而不牽乎卑辭之語，不奪乎從多之口。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage king regulates the age and controls custom, alone transforms on the potter's wheel above, and is not dragged by base words' speech, not seized by the mouths that follow the many.
translations.idiomatic: The sage king shapes the world as a potter turns his wheel: he is not tugged by petty rumor nor overruled by the clamor of the crowd.

sentences.zh: 故秦皇帝任中庶子蒙嘉之言，以信荊軻，而匕首竊發；

translations.literal: Therefore the Qin emperor trusted the words of the household son Meng Jia and believed Jing Ke, and the dagger struck secretly.
translations.idiomatic: The First Emperor believed his inner attendant Meng Jia and trusted Jing Ke—and the hidden dagger nearly struck home.

sentences.zh: 周文王獵涇渭，載呂尚歸，以王天下。

translations.literal: King Wen of Zhou hunted on the Jing and Wei, carried Lü Shang home, thereby to king the empire.
translations.idiomatic: King Wen hunted where the Jing meets the Wei, carried Lü Shang home in his carriage, and so came to rule the realm.

sentences.zh: 秦信左右而亡，周用烏集而王。

translations.literal: Qin trusted those at left and right and perished; Zhou employed flock-of-crows gathering and became king.
translations.idiomatic: Qin listened to palace favorites and fell; Zhou took counsel from chance-met strangers like a flock alighting and rose to kingship.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 以其能越攣拘之語，馳域外之議，獨觀乎昭曠之道也。

translations.literal: Because they were able to cross cramped and binding speech, gallop opinions beyond the boundary, alone gaze on the way of bright vastness.
translations.idiomatic: Because they could leap past narrow convention, hear voices from beyond the court, and keep their eyes on the clear, open path.

sentences.zh: 今人主沉謅諛之辭，牽帷□之制，使不羈之士與牛驥同皁，此鮑焦所以憤於世也。

translations.literal: Now the human lord sinks in fawning speech, is tied by curtain [lacuna] regulations, causes unbridled scholars to share the same stable with oxen and swift horses—this is why Bao Jiao was indignant toward the age.
translations.idiomatic: Today's rulers drown in flattery and let inner-chamber rules bind them; they pen free spirits with draft animals—no wonder Bao Jiao despaired of the world.

sentences.zh: 臣聞盛飾入朝者不以私污義，底厲名號者不以利傷行。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that those who enter court in full adornment do not by private interest stain righteousness; those who grind and polish name and title do not by profit injure conduct.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that one who comes to court in full dress does not stain his integrity with private gain, and one who hones a reputation does not sell his conduct for profit.

sentences.zh: 故里名勝母，曾子不入；

translations.literal: Therefore a village named 'Victory over Mother' Zengzi did not enter.
translations.idiomatic: There was a hamlet called 'Outshining Mother'—Zengzi would not set foot in it.

sentences.zh: 邑號朝歌，墨子回車。

translations.literal: A town called Zhaoge, Mozi turned his cart.
translations.idiomatic: There was a town called Zhaoge—Mozi turned his chariot around.

sentences.zh: 今欲使天下寥廓之士籠於威重之權，脅於位勢之貴，回面污行，以事諂諛之人，而求親近於左右，則士有伏死堀穴巖藪之中耳，安有盡忠信而趨闕下者哉！

translations.literal: Now if you desire to make vast scholars of the empire caged by the power of awe and majesty, threatened by the nobility of position and force, turn face and stain conduct, to serve fawning men, and seek closeness to those at left and right, then scholars will lie dead in burrowed caves and mountain thickets—how could there be those who exhaust loyalty and trust and hasten below the palace gate!
translations.idiomatic: If you cage the empire's freest minds with terror and rank, force them to grovel before sycophants and court favorites, they will die in their holes in the hills—who then would bring honest faith to your gate!

sentences.zh: 書奏孝王，孝王立出之，卒為上客。

translations.literal: The memorial was submitted to King Xiao; King Xiao immediately released him, in the end making him chief guest.
translations.idiomatic: When the memorial reached King Xiao, he had Zou Yang freed at once and later honored him as a chief retainer.

sentences.zh: 初，勝、詭欲使王求為漢嗣，王又嘗上書，願賜容車之地徑至長樂宮，自使梁國士眾築作甬道朝太后。

translations.literal: At first Sheng and Gui wished to make the king seek to be Han's heir; the king also once submitted a memorial, desiring to be granted land for a guest carriage road reaching straight to the Changle Palace, himself to send Liang state's soldiers and masses to build a covered passage to attend the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Yang Sheng and Gongsun Gui had urged the king to seek appointment as imperial heir; the king had also asked leave to run a private carriage road straight to Changle Palace and to raise a covered walk from Liang so he could visit the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 爰盎等皆建以為不可。

translations.literal: Yuan Ang and others all proposed holding it impossible.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Ang and others all argued this could not be allowed.

sentences.zh: 天子不許。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor refused.

sentences.zh: 梁王怒，令人刺殺盎。

translations.literal: King Liang was angry, ordered men to stab and kill Ang.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiao of Liang in his rage sent assassins to murder Yuan Ang.

sentences.zh: 上疑梁殺之，使者冠蓋相望責梁王。

translations.literal: The emperor suspected Liang killed him; envoys with caps and canopies followed one another reproaching King Liang.
translations.idiomatic: The throne suspected Liang; envoys with their train of carriages arrived one after another to call the king to account.

sentences.zh: 梁王始與勝、詭有謀，陽爭以為不可，故見讒。

translations.literal: King Liang at first had plotted with Sheng and Gui, Yang openly contended holding it impossible, therefore he was slandered.
translations.idiomatic: The king had plotted with Sheng and Gui, while Zou Yang had openly opposed the plot—so the king came under suspicion of slander.

sentences.zh: 枚先生、嚴夫子皆不敢諫。

translations.literal: Master Mei and Master Yan both did not dare remonstrate.
translations.idiomatic: Mei Sheng and Yan Ji dared not speak up.

sentences.zh: 及梁事敗，勝、詭死，孝王恐誅，乃思陽言，深辭謝之，繼以千金，令求方略解罪於上者，陽素知齊人王先生，年八十餘，多奇計，即往見，語以其事。

translations.literal: When the Liang affair failed, Sheng and Gui died; King Xiao feared execution, then thought of Yang's words, deeply declined and apologized, followed with a thousand in gold, ordered seeking of stratagems to resolve guilt toward the high one; Yang had long known a Qi man Master Wang, over eighty years old, with many strange plans, and immediately went to see him, telling him the affair.
translations.idiomatic: When the plot collapsed and Sheng and Gui were dead, King Xiao feared the death sentence, remembered Zou Yang's counsel, offered deep apologies and a thousand pounds of gold, and sent for a way to appease the throne. Zou Yang sought out the Qi strategist Master Wang, past eighty and full of stratagems, and laid the whole matter before him.

sentences.zh: 王先生曰：「難哉！

translations.literal: Master Wang said: "Hard!
translations.idiomatic: Master Wang said, "This is hard.

sentences.zh: 人主有私怨深怨，欲施必行之誅，誠難解也。

translations.literal: When the human lord has private resentment deep resentment, desiring to apply inevitable execution, truly it is hard to resolve.
translations.idiomatic: When a ruler nurses a settled grudge and means to carry out a certain execution, it is almost impossible to talk him round.

sentences.zh: 以太后之尊，骨肉之親，猶不能止，況臣下乎？

translations.literal: With the empress dowager's honor, flesh-and-bone closeness, still she could not stop it—how much the more ministers and subordinates!
translations.idiomatic: Not even the empress dowager's rank and kinship could stay him—what chance have mere subjects?

sentences.zh: 昔秦始皇有伏怒於太后，群臣諫而死者以十數。

translations.literal: In olden times the First Emperor of Qin had hidden anger toward the empress dowager; ministers who remonstrated and died were counted in tens.
translations.idiomatic: Qin Shihuang once nursed a secret rage against his mother; dozens of ministers died remonstrating.

sentences.zh: 得茅焦為廓大義，始皇非能說其言也，乃自強從之耳。

translations.literal: Obtaining Mao Jiao to enlarge righteousness, the First Emperor was not able to be pleased by his words; he only forced himself to follow them.
translations.idiomatic: Only when Mao Jiao framed the issue in high principle could the First Emperor yield—not because he liked the advice, but because he forced himself to accept it.

sentences.zh: 茅焦亦廑脫死如毛□耳，故事所以難者也。

translations.literal: Mao Jiao also barely escaped death like a hair [lacuna] ear; therefore affairs are what is hard.
translations.idiomatic: Mao Jiao himself escaped execution by a hair's breadth—that is how hard such moments are.

sentences.zh: 今子欲安之乎？」

translations.literal: Now do you desire to settle it?
translations.idiomatic: Do you mean to settle this now?

sentences.zh: 陽曰：「鄒、魯守經學，齊、楚多辯知，韓、魏時有奇節，吾將歷問之。」

translations.literal: Yang said: "Zou and Lu guard classical learning; Qi and Chu have much disputation and knowledge; Han and Wei at times have strange integrity; I will go and ask them in order."
translations.idiomatic: Zou Yang replied, "Zhou and Lu cling to the classics; Qi and Chu breed debaters; Han and Wei produce men of odd mettle—I will canvass them in turn."

sentences.zh: 王先生曰：「子行矣。

translations.literal: Master Wang said: "You go.
translations.idiomatic: Master Wang said, "Go then.

sentences.zh: 還，過我而西。」

translations.literal: When you return, pass me on the way west."
translations.idiomatic: On your way back west, stop here again."

sentences.zh: 鄒陽行月餘，莫能為謀，還，過王先生，曰：「臣將西矣，為如何？」

translations.literal: Zou Yang traveled over a month, none could make a plan; returning, he passed Master Wang and said: "Your servant is about to go west—what of it?"
translations.idiomatic: After more than a month no one had a plan. On his return Zou Yang called on Master Wang and said, "I am leaving for the capital—what now?"

sentences.zh: 王先生曰：「吾先日欲獻愚計，以為眾不可蓋，竊自薄陋不敢道也。

translations.literal: Master Wang said: "The other day I wished to offer a foolish plan, thinking the multitude could not be covered; I privately thought myself mean and shallow and did not dare speak.
