translations.idiomatic: Master Wang said, "I once had a humble scheme but held my tongue, thinking no common crowd could match it.

sentences.zh: 若子行，必往見王長君，士無過此者矣。」

translations.literal: If you go, you must go see Wang the elder brother of the king—among scholars none surpass this."
translations.idiomatic: When you go, you must see Wang Changjun, the empress's elder brother—there is no better man for this."

sentences.zh: 鄒陽發寤於心，曰：「敬諾。」

translations.literal: Yang awakened in heart and said: "Respectfully yes."
translations.idiomatic: Zou Yang's mind cleared; he said, "I obey."

sentences.zh: 辭去，不過梁，逕至長安，因客見王長君。

translations.literal: He took leave, did not pass through Liang, went straight to Chang'an, and through a client saw Wang Changjun.
translations.idiomatic: He avoided Liang, rode straight to Chang'an, and gained audience with Wang Changjun through a client.

sentences.zh: 初，吳王濞與七國謀反，及發，齊、濟北兩國城守不行。

translations.literal: At first King Liu Pi of Wu plotted rebellion with the seven states; when it broke out, Qi and Jibei two states defended their walls and did not march.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Pi of Wu rose with the seven kingdoms, Qi and Jibei held their walls and sent no troops.

sentences.zh: 漢既破吳，齊王自殺，不得立嗣。

translations.literal: After Han had broken Wu, the King of Qi killed himself and could not establish an heir.
translations.idiomatic: After Han crushed Wu, the King of Qi took his own life and left no heir.

sentences.zh: 濟北王亦欲自殺，幸全其妻子。

translations.literal: The King of Jibei also wished to kill himself, fortunately preserving his wife and children.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Jibei prepared to kill himself but was spared, with wife and children safe.

sentences.zh: 齊人公孫□謂濟北王曰：「臣請試為大王明說梁王，通意天子，說而不用。

translations.literal: A Qi man Gongsun [lacuna] said to the King of Jibei: "Your servant begs to try for the great king to clarify speech to the King of Liang, penetrate intent to the Son of Heaven; if persuaded and not employed, death is not yet late."
translations.idiomatic: A man of Qi, Gongsun [name missing], told the King of Jibei, "Let me try to explain your case to the King of Liang and win the emperor's ear; if he will not listen, you may die then—but not before."

sentences.zh: 死未晚也。」

translations.literal: Death is not yet late."
translations.idiomatic: It will not be too late to die."

sentences.zh: 公孫□遂見梁王，曰：「夫濟北之地，東接強齊，南牽吳、越，北脅燕、趙，此四分五裂之國，權不足以自守，勁不足以扞寇，又非有奇怪雲以待難也，雖墜言於吳，非其正計也。

translations.literal: Gongsun [lacuna] then saw the King of Liang and said: "As for Jibei's territory, east it joins strong Qi, south it is dragged by Wu and Yue, north it is threatened by Yan and Zhao—this is a state split four ways and cracked five ways; its power is insufficient to guard itself, its strength insufficient to repel invaders, nor does it have strange auspicious clouds to await calamity; though it let words fall toward Wu, that was not its proper plan.
translations.idiomatic: Gongsun [lacuna] then said to King Xiao, "Jibei lies between powerful Qi, Wu and Yue to the south, Yan and Zhao to the north—a splintered salient too weak to defend or strike, with no heavenly omens to shield it; even if it once made overtures to Wu, that was never its true design for rebellion.

sentences.zh: 昔者鄭祭仲許宋人立公子突以活其君，非義也，《春秋》記之，為其以生易死，以存易亡也。

translations.literal: In olden times Zhai Zhong of Zheng promised the Song men to establish Duke Tu to preserve his lord—not righteous; the Spring and Autumn records it, because he exchanged life for death, existence for perishing.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhai Zhong of Zheng promised the Song to set up Prince Tu to save his lord, the Spring and Autumn Annals recorded it—not as righteous, but because he traded life for death and survival for ruin.

sentences.zh: 鄉使濟北見情實，示不從之端，則吳必先歷齊畢濟北，招燕、趙而總之。

translations.literal: If formerly Jibei had shown true situation, displayed the beginnings of not following, then Wu would first have had to pass Qi and exhaust Jibei, summon Yan and Zhao and bind them.
translations.idiomatic: Had Jibei shown its true loyalty and refused the rebels early, Wu would first have had to fight through Qi and grind down Jibei before it could win Yan and Zhao to its league.

sentences.zh: 如此，則山東之從結而無隙矣。

translations.literal: In this way the Shandong league would have been tied and without crack.
translations.idiomatic: The eastern league would have held fast with no breach.

sentences.zh: 今吳、楚之王練諸侯之兵，驅白徒之眾，西與天子爭衡，濟北獨底節堅守不下。

translations.literal: Now the kings of Wu and Chu drill the feudal lords' troops, drive the masses of white followers, west to contend balance with the Son of Heaven; Jibei alone curbed pace and firmly defended and did not descend.
translations.idiomatic: While Wu and Chu drilled allied hosts and marched west against the throne, Jibei alone stood siege and never yielded.

sentences.zh: 使吳失與而無助，跬步獨進，瓦解土崩，破敗而不救者，未必非濟北之力也。

translations.literal: Causing Wu to lose allies and have no aid, half a step advancing alone, disintegrating like tile and earth collapsing, broken and defeated with none to save—this was not necessarily not Jibei's strength.
translations.idiomatic: Wu lost its partners and had no help; its advance faltered and its host crumbled—much of that collapse was Jibei's doing.

sentences.zh: 夫以區區之濟北而與諸侯爭強，是以羔犢之弱而扞虎狼之敵也。

translations.literal: As for using tiny Jibei to contend strength with the feudal lords, this is using lamb-calf weakness to ward tiger-wolf enemies.
translations.idiomatic: For that small state to defy the rebel alliance was like a lamb facing wolves.

sentences.zh: 守職不橈，可謂誠一矣。

translations.literal: Guarding duty without bending—this can be called sincere unity.
translations.idiomatic: It held its post without wavering—utter fidelity.

sentences.zh: 功義如此，尚見疑於上，脅肩低首，累足撫衿，使有自悔不前之心，非社稷之利也。

translations.literal: Merit and righteousness like this, still he was doubted by the high one, shrugging shoulders lowering head, piling feet stroking lapel, causing him to have a mind of self-repentance and not advancing—this is not profit to the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: For such service he was still suspected by the throne, left cringing and afraid to move—hardly a gain for the realm.

sentences.zh: 臣恐籓臣守職者疑之。

translations.literal: Your servant fears border ministers who guard duty will doubt it.
translations.idiomatic: I fear every loyal prince along the frontier will lose heart.

sentences.zh: 臣竊料之，能歷西山，徑長樂，抵未央，攘袂而正議者，獨大王耳。

translations.literal: Your servant privately measures it: able to cross the western hills, go straight to Changle, reach Weiyang, push sleeve and speak straight correction—only the great king.
translations.idiomatic: I believe only you, my lord, can cross the western hills, pass Changle, reach Weiyang Palace, roll up your sleeve, and speak plain truth to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上有全亡之功，下有安百姓之名，德淪於骨髓，恩加於無窮，願大王留意詳惟之。」

translations.literal: Above there is the merit of complete preservation, below there is the name of pacifying the hundred surnames; virtue soaks into marrow and bone, grace extends without end—may the great king take note and examine carefully."
translations.idiomatic: You would save a kingdom above and quiet the people below; your virtue would sink into bone and your kindness know no end—I beg you to weigh this carefully."

sentences.zh: 孝王大說，使人馳以聞。

translations.literal: King Xiao was greatly pleased, ordered men to gallop and report it.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiao was delighted and sent riders to lay the plea before the court.

sentences.zh: 濟北王得不坐，徙封於淄川。

translations.literal: The King of Jibei obtained not to sit guilty, was moved enfeoffment to Zichuan.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Jibei escaped punishment and was transferred to a fief at Zichuan.

sentences.zh: 枚乘字叔，淮陽人也，為吳王濞郎中。

translations.literal: Mei Cheng styled Shu, a man of Huaiyang, served as gentleman to King Liu Pi of Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Mei Sheng, styled Shu, came from Huaiyang and was a gentleman-attendant to King Liu Pi of Wu.

sentences.zh: 吳王之初怨望謀為逆也，乘奏書諫曰：

translations.literal: When the King of Wu at first harbored resentment and plotted to be rebellious, Cheng submitted a memorial remonstrating, saying:
translations.idiomatic: When the king first nursed a grudge and plotted revolt, Mei Sheng sent up this memorial:

sentences.zh: 臣聞得全者全昌，失全者全亡。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that he who obtains completeness wholly flourishes, he who loses completeness wholly perishes.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that wholeness brings full fortune, and its loss brings utter ruin.

sentences.zh: 舜無立錐之地，以有天下；

translations.literal: Shun had no ground the size of a awl-point, thereby possessed the empire.
translations.idiomatic: Shun began without an inch of land yet came to own the realm.

sentences.zh: 禹無十戶之聚，以王諸侯。

translations.literal: Yu had no gathering of ten households, thereby to king the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Yu had not ten households to his name yet came to be king over the feudal lords.

sentences.zh: 湯、武之士不過百里，上不絕三光之明，下不傷百姓之心者，有王術也。

translations.literal: The scholars of Tang and Wu did not exceed a hundred li; above they did not cut off the three luminaries' brightness, below they did not injure the hundred surnames' hearts—because they had the art of kingship.
translations.idiomatic: The founders of Shang and Zhou began on a patch of ground no larger than a hundred li, yet they never dimmed sun, moon, and stars above nor broke the people's hearts below—because they held the true art of kingship.

sentences.zh: 故父子之道，天性也；

translations.literal: Therefore the way of father and son is heaven's nature.
translations.idiomatic: The bond of father and son is nature itself.

sentences.zh: 忠臣不避重誅以直諫，則事無遺策，功流萬世。

translations.literal: If a loyal minister does not shrink from heavy punishment to remonstrate straight, then affairs lack leftover stratagems and merit flows ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: When a loyal minister risks death to speak plainly, no policy is left unexamined and his fame runs for ages.

sentences.zh: 臣乘願披心腹而效愚忠，唯大王少加意念惻怛之心於臣乘言。

translations.literal: Your servant Cheng wishes to spread heart and belly and offer foolish loyalty; only may the great king slightly apply a mind of pity and grief to your servant Cheng's words.
translations.idiomatic: I, Cheng, lay bare my heart and offer my poor loyalty; I beg you, my lord, to hear me with compassion.

sentences.zh: 夫以一縷之任系千鈞之重，上縣無極之高，下垂不測之淵，雖甚愚之人猶知哀其將絕也。

translations.literal: As for using one thread's bearing to tie a thousand jun's weight, above suspended without limit's height, below hanging unfathomable's abyss—though very foolish people still know to pity its about to break.
translations.idiomatic: It is like hanging a thousand jun from a single thread over a bottomless pit—even a fool knows to fear the snap.

sentences.zh: 馬方駭鼓而驚之，系方絕又重鎮之；

translations.literal: The horses just startled, the drum beats and startles them; the cord just about to break again heavily presses it.
translations.idiomatic: The team is already panicked, yet you beat the drum; the rope is fraying, yet you pile on more weight.

sentences.zh: 系絕於天下不可復結，隊入深淵難以復出。

translations.literal: The cord breaks under the empire, cannot again be tied; falling into the deep abyss, hard again to come out.
translations.idiomatic: Once the thread parts it cannot be knotted again; once you fall into the pit you cannot climb back.

sentences.zh: 其出不出，間不容髮。

translations.literal: Its coming out or not coming out, between them a hair does not contain.
translations.idiomatic: Between safety and ruin there is not room for a hair.

sentences.zh: 能聽忠臣之言，百舉必脫。

translations.literal: If able to listen to a loyal minister's words, a hundred moves must escape.
translations.idiomatic: Heed a loyal minister and you may slip free a hundred times over.

sentences.zh: 必若所欲為，危於累卵，難於上天；

translations.literal: If it must be as the desires wish to act, more dangerous than piled eggs, harder than ascending heaven.
translations.idiomatic: If you insist on your present course, you stand higher than a tower of eggs and face a climb harder than heaven.

sentences.zh: 變所欲為，易於反掌，安於泰山。

translations.literal: Change what the desires wish to act, easier than turning the palm, safer than Mount Tai.
translations.idiomatic: Change course and the danger becomes as easy as turning your hand, the safety as sure as Mount Tai.

sentences.zh: 今欲極天命之壽，敝無窮之樂，究萬乘之勢，不出反掌之易，以居泰山之安，而欲乘累卵之危，走上天之難，此愚臣之所大惑也。

translations.literal: Now desiring to exhaust Heaven's mandate's years, exhaust endless joy, exhaust ten thousand chariots' power, not going out of turning the palm's ease, to dwell in Mount Tai's safety, yet desiring to ride piled eggs' danger, walk ascending heaven's difficulty—this is what your foolish servant greatly doubts.
translations.idiomatic: You would spend heaven's allotted years and the power of ten thousand chariots chasing boundless pleasure, yet refuse the ease of a hand's turn and the safety of Taishan while you mount the peril of stacked eggs and the climb to the sky—this your humble servant cannot understand.

sentences.zh: 人性有畏其景而惡其跡者，卻背而走，跡愈多，景愈疾，不知就陰而止，景滅跡絕。

translations.literal: Human nature has those who fear their shadow and hate their tracks; they turn back and walk away, yet tracks grow more, shadow grows more swift, not knowing to go to shade and stop, then shadow ends and tracks cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Some men fear their shadow and loathe their footprints: they run from the sun and only multiply both—never thinking to step into shade, where shadow dies and prints end.

sentences.zh: 欲人勿聞，莫若勿言；

translations.literal: Desiring others not to hear—nothing like not speaking.
translations.idiomatic: If you would not be overheard, better not speak.

sentences.zh: 欲人勿知，莫若勿為。

translations.literal: Desiring others not to know—nothing like not doing.
translations.idiomatic: If you would not be found out, better not do the deed.

sentences.zh: 欲湯之凔，一人炊之，百人揚之，無益也，不如絕薪止火而已。

translations.literal: Desiring soup's cool—one person cooks it, a hundred people fan it, without benefit; not like cutting firewood and stopping fire only.
translations.idiomatic: To cool boiling soup one man heats while a hundred fan—useless; better lift the fuel and kill the fire.

sentences.zh: 不絕之於彼，而救之於此，譬猶抱薪而救火也。

translations.literal: Not cutting it off there yet saving it here is like embracing firewood to save from fire.
translations.idiomatic: To stoke the blaze here while hoping to douse it there is to hug kindling to a bonfire.

sentences.zh: 養由基，楚之善射者也，去楊葉百步，百發百中。

translations.literal: Yang Youji was Chu state's good archer; he went a hundred paces from the poplar leaf, a hundred shots a hundred hits.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Youji of Chu could stand a hundred paces from a willow leaf and hit it a hundred times in a hundred tries.

sentences.zh: 楊葉之大，加百中焉，可謂善射矣。

translations.literal: The poplar leaf's size—adding a hundred hits to it—can be called good at shooting.
translations.idiomatic: To put a hundred arrows through a leaf as small as a willow blade is indeed fine shooting.

sentences.zh: 然其所止，乃百步之內耳，比於臣乘，未知操弓持矢也。

translations.literal: Yet what he stopped at was only within a hundred paces; compared to your servant Cheng, not yet knowing to grasp bow and hold arrow.
translations.idiomatic: Yet he never shot beyond a hundred paces; set beside your servant Cheng, he has not yet learned to take up bow and arrow.

sentences.zh: 福生有基，禍生有胎；

translations.literal: Fortune birth has a foundation, calamity birth has a fetus.
translations.idiomatic: Good fortune has its roots; disaster has its embryo.

sentences.zh: 納其基，絕其胎，禍何自來？

translations.literal: Accept its foundation, cut off its fetus—how does calamity come of itself?
translations.idiomatic: Nourish the root and cut the bud—how should calamity arise?

sentences.zh: 泰山之霤穿石，單極之絲亢斷□。

translations.literal: Mount Tai's dripping wears through stone; a single pole's thread [lacuna] breaks [lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: The drip from Taishan bores through stone; a single rope's strand can snap the beam.

sentences.zh: 水非石之鑽，索非木之鋸，漸靡使之然也。

translations.literal: Water is not stone's drill, cord is not wood's saw—gradual wearing makes it so.
translations.idiomatic: Water is no chisel and rope no saw—yet grinding wear does the work.

sentences.zh: 夫銖銖而稱之，至石必差；

translations.literal: As for weighing by zhu zhu, reaching a stone must miss.
translations.idiomatic: Weigh grain by the scruple and you will miss the stone.

sentences.zh: 寸寸而度之，至丈必過。

translations.literal: Measuring cun cun, reaching a zhang must pass.
translations.idiomatic: Measure inch by inch and you will overshoot the rod.

sentences.zh: 石稱丈量，逕而寡失。

translations.literal: Stone weighed and zhang measured, straight and little loss.
translations.idiomatic: Weigh by the stone and measure by the rod—straightforward and seldom wrong.

sentences.zh: 夫十圍之木，始生如薛，足可搔而絕，手可擢而拔，據其未生，先其未形也。

translations.literal: As for a tree ten arm-spans around, at birth it was like shepherd's purse; feet could scratch and break it, hands could pluck and pull it up—because one seized it not yet born, before it not yet formed.
translations.idiomatic: A tree that will take ten men to gird began as a sprig you could break with a toe—catch evil before it grows.

sentences.zh: 磨礱底厲，不見其損，有時而盡；

translations.literal: Grinding and polishing, bottom sharpening, not seeing its loss, there is a time when it is exhausted.
translations.idiomatic: Whetstone and grindstone wear away unseen—until the blade is gone.

sentences.zh: 種樹畜養，不見其益，有時而大；

translations.literal: Planting trees raising nurture, not seeing its increase, there is a time when it is great.
translations.idiomatic: Plant a tree and tend it; you see no growth day by day—until one day it towers.

sentences.zh: 積德累行，不知其善，有時而用；

translations.literal: Accumulating virtue piling conduct, not knowing its good, there is a time when it is employed.
translations.idiomatic: Heap up merit in secret; you may not notice the good—until the moment it saves you.

sentences.zh: 棄義背理，不知其惡，有時而亡。

translations.literal: Casting aside righteousness turning back on principle, not knowing its evil, there is a time when it perishes.
translations.idiomatic: Cast off right and turn your back on principle; you may not feel the evil—until the hour of ruin.

sentences.zh: 臣願大王孰計而身行之，此百世不易之道也。

translations.literal: Your servant hopes the great king will examine the plan and personally practice it; this is the way not changed in a hundred generations.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you, my lord, to weigh this counsel and live by it—the changeless path for a hundred generations.

sentences.zh: 吳王不納。

translations.literal: The King of Wu did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Wu would not listen.

sentences.zh: 乘等去而之梁，從孝王游。

translations.literal: Cheng and the others left and went to Liang, roaming with King Xiao.
translations.idiomatic: Mei Sheng and his companions left for Liang and joined King Xiao's court.

sentences.zh: 景帝即位，御史大夫晃錯為漢定制度，損削諸侯，吳王遂與六國謀反，舉兵西鄉，以誅錯為名。

translations.literal: When Emperor Jing took the throne, Imperial Counselor Chao Cuo for Han fixed the regulations, diminished and cut the feudal lords; the King of Wu thereupon plotted rebellion with six states, raised troops westward, in the name of executing Chao Cuo.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing had ascended; Grand Counselor Chao Cuo rewrote Han law and pared the kingdoms, so the King of Wu conspired with six states, marched west, and used "punishing Chao Cuo" as his banner.

sentences.zh: 漢聞之，斬錯以謝諸侯。

translations.literal: Han heard it, beheaded Chao Cuo to apologize to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: The court cut off Chao Cuo's head to appease the kings.

sentences.zh: 枚乘復說吳王曰：

translations.literal: Mei Cheng again persuaded the King of Wu, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Mei Sheng remonstrated again:

sentences.zh: 昔者，秦西舉胡戎之難，北備榆中之關，南距羌□之塞，東當六國之從。

translations.literal: In olden times, Qin raised difficulty with the Hu and Rong in the west, prepared Yuzhong's pass in the north, blocked the Qiang [lacuna] barrier in the south, faced east the six states' league.
translations.idiomatic: Qin once fought the western Rong, guarded the Yuzhong pass in the north, held the Qiang passes in the south, and turned east to meet the allied six states.

sentences.zh: 六國乘信陵之籍，明蘇秦之約，厲荊軻之威，並力一心以備秦。

translations.literal: The six states rode on Lord Xinling's credentials, clarified Su Qin's covenant, sharpened Jing Ke's awe, combined strength one heart to prepare against Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Those six kingdoms rode on Lord Xinling's name, honored Su Qin's league, brandished Jing Ke's daring, and stood as one against Qin.

sentences.zh: 然秦卒禽六國，滅其社稷，而並天下，是何也？

translations.literal: Yet Qin in the end captured the six states, extinguished their altars of soil and grain, and annexed the empire—why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Yet Qin swallowed them all, snuffed their dynastic fires, and unified the realm—why?

sentences.zh: 則地利不同，而民輕重不等也。

translations.literal: Then terrain's profit was not the same, and the people's light and heavy were not equal.
translations.idiomatic: Because terrain differed and the strength of their peoples was not the same.

sentences.zh: 今漢據全秦之地，兼六國之眾，修戎狄之義，而南朝羌□，此其與秦，地相什而民相百，大王之所明知也。

translations.literal: Now Han occupies all Qin's land, combines the six states' multitudes, cultivates righteousness with Rong and Di, and faces south the Qiang [lacuna]—this compared to Qin, terrain is tenfold and people a hundredfold—the great king knows it clearly.
translations.idiomatic: Han holds all Qin's old territory and the combined manpower of the six states, courts the Rong and Di in good faith, and fronts the Qiang in the south—its ground outstrips Qin's ten times over, its people a hundredfold, as you well know.

sentences.zh: 今夫讒諛之臣為大王計者，不論骨肉之義，民之輕重，國之大小，以為吳禍，此臣所以為大王患也。

translations.literal: Now as for fawning ministers who plan for the great king, they do not discuss flesh-and-bone duty, the people's light and heavy, the state's large and small, and take it as Wu's calamity—this is why your servant takes it as the great king's worry.
translations.idiomatic: Yet your flatterers plot for you without regard to kinship, popular strength, or the size of states, and call it Wu's doom—that is what I fear for you.

sentences.zh: 夫舉吳兵以訾於漢，璧猶蠅蚋之附群牛，腐肉之齒利劍，鋒接必無事矣。

translations.literal: As for raising Wu troops to threaten Han, it is like flies and gnats attaching to a herd of oxen, rotten flesh meeting sharp sword—when edges meet there will surely be nothing.
translations.idiomatic: To march Wu against Han is like gnats swarming a herd of oxen or rotten meat meeting a keen blade—when steel strikes, you will be nothing.

sentences.zh: 天子聞吳率失職諸侯，願責先帝之遺約，今漢親誅其三公，以謝前過，是大王之威加於天下，而功越於湯、武也。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven heard Wu leading feoffees who had lost their stations, desired to call to account the late emperor's bequeathed agreements; now Han personally executed its three high ministers to apologize for former faults—this is the great king's awe applied to the empire, and merit surpassing Tang and Wu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor heard Wu lead disaffected kings and invoke the late emperor's covenants; the court has just executed three grand ministers to atone for past wrongs—your prestige, my lord, already weighs on the realm, your deed outshines Tang and Wu.

sentences.zh: 夫吳有諸侯之位，而實富於天子；

translations.literal: As for Wu, it has a feudal lord's rank, yet in reality is richer than the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Wu holds only a king's title, yet it is wealthier than the throne.

sentences.zh: 有隱匿之名，而居過於中國。

translations.literal: It has the name of concealment, yet dwells beyond the Central States.
translations.idiomatic: It is called a remote fief, yet its splendor exceeds the heartland.

sentences.zh: 夫漢並二十四郡，十七諸侯，方輸錯出，運行數千里不絕於道，其珍怪不如東山之府。

translations.literal: As for Han, annexing twenty-four commanderies and seventeen feudal lords, tribute from all quarters crosses and emerges, transport runs several thousand li without break on the roads—its treasures and marvels do not match the Eastern Mountain treasury.
translations.idiomatic: Han gathers tribute from twenty-four commanderies and seventeen kingdoms along roads that never rest—yet its hoard cannot match your Eastern Mountain vaults.

sentences.zh: 轉粟西鄉，陸行不絕，水行滿河，不如海陵之倉。

translations.literal: Turning grain westward, land travel without break, water travel filling the rivers—does not match Hailing's granaries.
translations.idiomatic: Its grain barges fill the rivers—yet they cannot match your granaries at Hailing.

sentences.zh: 修治上林，雜以離宮，積聚玩好，圈守禽獸，不如長洲之苑。

translations.literal: Repairing and ordering Shanglin, mixing detached palaces, piling rare delights, enclosing and guarding birds and beasts—does not match the Changzhou park.
translations.idiomatic: Its Shanglin Park with its lodges and menageries cannot match your Changzhou hunting preserve.

sentences.zh: 游曲台，臨上路，不如朝夕之池。

translations.literal: Roaming the Curved Terrace, overlooking the high road—does not match the morning-and-evening pool.
translations.idiomatic: Its Curved Terrace and highways cannot match your pleasure pools at hand.

sentences.zh: 深壁高壘，副以關城，不如江淮之險。

translations.literal: Deep walls high ramparts, doubled with pass cities—do not match the Yangtze and Huai's peril.
translations.idiomatic: Its stacked walls and barred passes cannot match the natural rampart of the Yangtze and Huai.

sentences.zh: 此臣之所為大王樂也。

translations.literal: This is what your servant takes as the great king's joy.
translations.idiomatic: That, my lord, is your advantage.

sentences.zh: 今大王還兵疾歸，尚得十半。

translations.literal: Now if the great king turns the army and quickly returns, still can obtain ten's half.
translations.idiomatic: Wheel your host about now and you may yet keep half of what you have.

sentences.zh: 不然，漢知吳之有吞天下之心也，赫然加怒，遣羽林黃頭循江而下，龔大王之都；

translations.literal: If not so, Han knows Wu has the mind to swallow the empire; glaringly adding anger, it will dispatch feathered forest and yellow-head guards down the river, to strike the great king's capital.
translations.idiomatic: Refuse, and once Han knows you mean to seize the realm, it will blaze with wrath and send palace guards and river fleets down the stream against your capital.

sentences.zh: 魯東海絕吳之餉道；

translations.literal: Lu and Donghai cut Wu's supply road.
translations.idiomatic: Lu and Donghai will sever your supply lines.

sentences.zh: 梁王飭車騎，習戰射，積粟固守，以備滎陽，待吳之饑。

translations.literal: The King of Liang arrays chariots and horse, practices war and shooting, piles grain and firmly guards, to prepare Xingyang, awaiting Wu's hunger.
translations.idiomatic: King Xiao of Liang will drill cavalry, stack grain, and hold Xingyang fast until Wu starves.

sentences.zh: 大王雖欲反都，亦不得已。

translations.literal: Though the great king desires to return to the capital, also he will have no means.
translations.idiomatic: Then even if you wish to wheel back to your seat, you will be unable.

sentences.zh: 夫三淮南之計不負其約，齊王殺身以滅其跡，四國不得出兵其郡，趙囚邯鄲，此不可掩，亦已明矣。

translations.literal: As for the three Huainan princes' plan not betraying their covenant, the King of Qi killing himself to extinguish his traces, the four states not obtaining to send troops from their commanderies, Zhao imprisoned at Handan—these cannot be covered, also already clear.
translations.idiomatic: The Huainan princes kept faith, the King of Qi died to wipe away suspicion, four kingdoms never marched, Zhao is bottled in Handan—these facts cannot be hidden; they are plain.

sentences.zh: 大王已去千里之國，而制於十里之內矣。

translations.literal: The great king has already left a state of a thousand li, yet is controlled within ten li.
translations.idiomatic: You have marched out of a thousand-li realm only to be pinned within ten li.

sentences.zh: 張、韓將此地，弓高宿左右，兵不得下壁，軍不得太息，臣竊哀之。

translations.literal: Zhang and Han will command this place, Gongao camps at left and right, troops cannot descend the walls, armies cannot greatly breathe—your servant privately pities it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang and Han will hold this ground, Gongao will camp on your flanks—your men will not dare leave the walls nor draw a full breath; I grieve for it.

sentences.zh: 願大王孰察焉。

translations.literal: I hope the great king examines it carefully.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to weigh this.

sentences.zh: 吳王不用乘策，卒見禽滅。

translations.literal: The King of Wu did not use Cheng's stratagem, in the end was captured and destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: The king ignored Mei Sheng's plan and was taken and ruined.

sentences.zh: 漢既平七國，乘由是知名。

translations.literal: After Han had pacified the seven states, Cheng thereby became famous.
translations.idiomatic: When Han crushed the seven kingdoms, Mei Sheng's name was known everywhere.

sentences.zh: 景帝召拜乘為弘農都尉。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing summoned and appointed Cheng as Metropolitan Commandant of Hongnong.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing summoned him and named him chief of Hongnong commandery.

sentences.zh: 乘久為大國上賓，與英俊並游，得其所好，不樂郡吏，以病去官。

translations.literal: Cheng long was a great state's chief guest, roamed with the heroic and outstanding, obtained what he liked, did not delight in commandery clerks, by illness left office.
translations.idiomatic: Long a honored client of a great kingdom, keeping company with the brilliant, he found no joy in a county post and resigned on grounds of illness.

sentences.zh: 復游梁，梁客皆善屬辭賦，乘尤高。

translations.literal: Again he roamed Liang; Liang's guests all were good at composing verse rhapsodies; Cheng was especially high.
translations.idiomatic: He returned to Liang, where every retainer wrote fu; Mei Sheng stood highest among them.

sentences.zh: 孝王薨，乘歸淮陰。

translations.literal: When King Xiao died, Cheng returned to Huaiyin.
translations.idiomatic: After King Xiao died, Mei Sheng went back to his home commandery of Huaiyin.

sentences.zh: 武帝自為太子聞乘名，及即位，乘年老，乃以安車蒲輪征乘，道死。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu when himself heir-apparent had heard Cheng's name; when he mounted the throne, Cheng was old; then with cushioned cart and rattan wheels he summoned Cheng; Cheng died on the road.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor had known Mei Sheng's name since his own heirship; after his accession, with the scholar's carriage and rattan wheels he called the aged Mei Sheng, who died en route.

sentences.zh: 詔問乘子，無能為文者，後乃得其薛子皋。

translations.literal: He edicted asking for Cheng's sons; none could compose literature; afterward then obtained his bastard son Gao.
translations.idiomatic: An edict sought Mei Sheng's sons; none could write; at last a bastard son, Gao, was found.

sentences.zh: 子 皋

translations.literal: Son Gao
translations.idiomatic: His son: Mei Gao

sentences.zh: 皋字少孺，乘在梁時，取皋母為小妻。

translations.literal: Gao styled Shaoru; when Cheng was in Liang, he took Gao's mother as a minor wife.
translations.idiomatic: Mei Gao, styled Shaoru: while Mei Sheng was in Liang he took Gao's mother as a concubine.

sentences.zh: 乘之東歸也，皋母不肯隨乘，乘怒，分皋數千錢，留與母居。

translations.literal: When Cheng returned east, Gao's mother was unwilling to follow Cheng; Cheng was angry, divided several thousand cash to Gao, left him to dwell with his mother.
translations.idiomatic: When Mei Sheng went east, the mother refused to follow; in anger he gave the boy a few thousand cash and left him with her.

sentences.zh: 年十七，上書梁共王，得召為郎。

translations.literal: At age seventeen he submitted a memorial to King Gong of Liang, was summoned and became a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: At seventeen he memorialized King Gong of Liang and was called to court as a gentleman.

sentences.zh: 三年，為王使，與冗從爭，見讒惡遇罪，家室沒入。

translations.literal: In three years, serving as the king's envoy, he quarreled with supernumerary followers, was slandered and met crime, household and wives were confiscated.
translations.idiomatic: Within three years, on mission for the king, he brawled with hangers-on, was slandered, convicted, and his family confiscated.

sentences.zh: 皋亡至長安。

translations.literal: Gao fled to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Mei Gao fled to Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 會赦，上書北闕，自陳枚乘之子。

translations.literal: Meeting an amnesty, he submitted a memorial at the north gate, himself stating he was Mei Cheng's son.
translations.idiomatic: When an amnesty came, he petitioned at the northern portal, declaring himself Mei Sheng's son.

sentences.zh: 上得大喜，召入見待詔，皋因賦殿中。

translations.literal: The emperor obtained it and was greatly pleased, summoned him in to see and await edict; Gao thereupon composed a fu in the hall.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor rejoiced, summoned him as a palace writer-in-waiting, and Mei Gao composed a rhapsody in the hall.

sentences.zh: 詔使賦平樂館，善之。

translations.literal: He edicted ordering him to compose on the Pingle Lodge; he approved it.
translations.idiomatic: He was ordered to write on Pingle Lodge and won praise.

sentences.zh: 拜為郎，使匈奴。

translations.literal: He was appointed gentleman and sent to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: He was made a gentleman-cavalier and sent as envoy to the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 皋不通經術，詼笑類俳倡，為賦頌好嫚戲，以故得媟默貴幸，比東方朔、郭舍人等，而不得比嚴助等得尊官。

translations.literal: Gao did not penetrate classical learning; jest and laughter resembled jesters; composing fu and eulogies he liked lewd sport; therefore he obtained frivolous intimacy and honored favor, comparable to Dongfang Shuo and Guo She'er and others, yet could not compare to Yan Zhu and others in obtaining honored office.
translations.idiomatic: Mei Gao had no classical learning; his humor was buffoon's humor; his fu and songs ran to coarse jokes—so he won the emperor's intimate favor like Dongfang Shuo and Guo She'er, but never rose to grave posts like Yan Zhu.

sentences.zh: 武帝春秋二十九乃得皇子，群臣喜，故皋與東方朔作《皇太子生賦》及《立皇子禖祝》，受詔所為，皆不從故事，重皇子也。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu at age twenty-nine then obtained a imperial son; the host of ministers rejoiced; therefore Gao and Dongfang Shuo composed "Fu on the Birth of the Imperial Heir" and "Prayer for Establishing the Imperial Son"; receiving edict what they composed, all did not follow old precedents, emphasizing the imperial son.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor was twenty-nine and at last had a son, the court rejoiced; Mei Gao and Dongfang Shuo were commissioned to write "On the Birth of the Heir" and "Prayer for the Heir's Rite"—compositions that broke precedent to magnify the event.

sentences.zh: 初，衛皇后立，皋奏賦以戒終。

translations.literal: At first when Empress Wei was established, Gao submitted a fu to warn of the end.
translations.idiomatic: When Empress Wei was first raised, Mei Gao submitted a fu warning how such stories end.

sentences.zh: 皋為賦善於朔也。

translations.literal: Gao in composing fu was better than Shuo.
translations.idiomatic: In fu composition Mei Gao surpassed Dongfang Shuo.

sentences.zh: 從行至甘泉、雍、河東，東巡狩，封泰山，塞決河宣房，遊觀三輔離宮館，臨山澤，弋獵射馭狗馬蹴鞠刻鏤，上有所感，輒使賦之。

translations.literal: Following the progress to Ganquan, Yong, Hedong, east on inspection tour, enfeoffing Mount Tai, blocking the burst river at Xuanfang, roaming and viewing the three adjuncts' detached palaces and lodges, approaching mountains and marshes, hunting and shooting, driving dogs and horses, kicking ball, carving and inlaying—when the emperor had a feeling, then he ordered fu on it.
translations.idiomatic: He accompanied the court to Ganquan, Yong, Hedong, on the eastern tour, the Mount Tai rite, the Xuanfang works on the Yellow River, the lodges of the capital region—hunting, racing dogs, ball games, inlay work—whenever the emperor felt moved, Mei Gao was told to write.

sentences.zh: 為文疾，受詔輒成，故所賦者多。

translations.literal: He was quick at writing; receiving edict then completed—therefore what he composed was many.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote at speed and finished whatever he was ordered—hence his large body of work.

sentences.zh: 司馬相如善為文而遲，故所作少而善於皋。

translations.literal: Sima Xiangru was good at writing yet slow; therefore what he made was few yet better than Gao's.
translations.idiomatic: Sima Xiangru wrote more slowly but better—so he produced less yet finer pieces than Mei Gao.

sentences.zh: 皋賦辭中自言為賦不如相如，又言為賦乃俳，見視如倡，自悔類倡也。

translations.literal: In Gao's fu diction he himself said composing fu was not as good as Xiangru, again said composing fu was jesting, was viewed like jesters, himself regretted resembling jesters.
translations.idiomatic: In his own fu Mei Gao admitted he fell short of Sima Xiangru, called the art mere clowning, said he was treated like a player, and regretted resembling one.

sentences.zh: 故其賦有詆□東方朔，又自詆□。

translations.literal: Therefore his fu had slander [lacuna] Dongfang Shuo, again self-slander [lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: His pieces therefore mocked Dongfang Shuo and mocked himself.

sentences.zh: 其文骫骳，曲隨其事，皆得其意，頗詼笑，不甚閒靡。

translations.literal: His text was bent and broken, crookedly following each affair, all obtained its meaning, rather jesting, not very polished and extravagant.
translations.idiomatic: His style was rough and wayward, tailored to each occasion, apt to the point, often humorous, seldom ornate.

sentences.zh: 凡可讀者百二十篇，其尤女曼戲不可讀者尚數十篇。

translations.literal: In all, readable ones number one hundred twenty pieces; those especially lewd and sportive unreadable still number several tens of pieces.
translations.idiomatic: About a hundred twenty pieces are fit to read; several dozen more are too obscene to circulate.

sentences.zh: 路溫舒

translations.literal: Lu Wenshu
translations.idiomatic: Lu Wenshu

sentences.zh: 路溫舒字長君，巨鹿東里人也。

translations.literal: Lu Wenshu styled Changjun, a man of Julu's eastern hamlet.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Wenshu, styled Changjun, came from the eastern ward of Julu.

sentences.zh: 父為裡監門。

translations.literal: His father was village gate watchman.
translations.idiomatic: His father was gatekeeper for the village.

sentences.zh: 使溫舒牧羊，溫舒取澤中蒲，截以為牒，編用寫書。

translations.literal: He ordered Wenshu to herd sheep; Wenshu took cattail from the marsh, cut it to make slips, wove them to use for writing books.
translations.idiomatic: He sent the boy to tend sheep; Lu Wenshu cut marsh cattails into slips and copied texts on them.

sentences.zh: 稍習善，求為獄小吏，因學律令，轉為獄史，縣中疑事皆問焉。

translations.literal: Gradually practicing well, he sought to be prison petty clerk, thereby studied statutes and orders, turned to be prison clerk; doubtful matters in the county all asked him.
translations.idiomatic: As he improved he became a prison runner, studied law, rose to prison clerk, and the county brought him every doubtful case.

sentences.zh: 太守行縣，見而異之，署決曹史。

translations.literal: When the grand administrator inspected the county, saw him and thought him unusual, appointed him clerk of the decision bureau.
translations.idiomatic: The grand administrator, touring the county, marked him out and named him clerk of the sentencing office.

sentences.zh: 又受《春秋》，通大義。

translations.literal: Again he received the Spring and Autumn, penetrated great meaning.
translations.idiomatic: He also mastered the Spring and Autumn and grasped its larger principles.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，為山邑丞，坐法免，復為郡吏。

translations.literal: Recommended filial and incorrupt, became assistant magistrate of Shanyi, sat on law dismissed, again became commandery clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as filial and incorrupt, he served as assistant at Shanyi, lost his post for a legal fault, then returned as a commandery clerk.

sentences.zh: 臣聞齊有無知之禍，而桓公以興；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Qi had Wuzhi's calamity, yet Duke Huan thereby rose.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that after Qi's crisis of Wuzhi, Duke Huan rose from it.

sentences.zh: 晉有驪姬之難，而文公用伯。

translations.literal: Jin had Lady Li's difficulty, yet Duke Wen used it to become hegemon.
translations.idiomatic: Jin suffered Lady Li's plot, yet Duke Wen turned it into hegemony.

sentences.zh: 近世趙王不終，諸呂作亂，而孝文為太宗。

translations.literal: In recent ages the Zhao king did not reach his end, the various Lüs made rebellion, yet Emperor Xiaowen became Grand Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: In our day the King of Zhao met an untimely end, the Lü clan rebelled, and Emperor Wen became founding ancestor.

sentences.zh: 繇是觀之，禍亂之作，將以開聖人也。

translations.literal: From this viewing it, misfortune and rebellion's arising will be to open the sage man.
translations.idiomatic: From this it follows that rebellion and disaster are heaven's way of ushering in a sage.

sentences.zh: 故桓、文扶微興壞，尊文武之業，澤加百姓，功潤諸侯，雖不及三王，天下歸仁焉。

translations.literal: Therefore Huan and Wen supported the slight and raised the ruined, honored Wen and Wu's enterprise, grace extended to the hundred surnames, merit moistened the feudal lords; though they did not reach the Three Kings, the empire returned to humaneness.
translations.idiomatic: Thus Huan and Wen raised fallen houses, continued the work of Wen and Wu, blessed the people and strengthened the nobles; though they fell short of the Three Kings, the realm turned to humaneness.

sentences.zh: 文帝永思至德，以承天心，崇仁義，省刑罰，通關梁，一遠近，敬賢如大賓，愛民如赤子，內恕情之所安，而施之於海內，是以囹圄空虛，天下太平。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen forever pondered utmost virtue, to receive heaven's mind, exalted benevolence and righteousness, reduced punishments and penalties, opened passes and bridges, unified far and near, respected the worthy as great guests, loved the people as newborn children, inwardly applied what feeling found peace, and extended it within the seas—therefore prisons were empty and void, the empire was grandly peaceful.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen pondered supreme virtue to answer heaven: he honored benevolence, lightened punishments, opened the roads, treated worthy men as honored guests and the people as infants, and spread inward peace across the seas—so his jails stood empty and the realm knew great peace.

sentences.zh: 夫繼變化之後，必有異舊之恩，此賢聖所以昭天命也。

translations.literal: As for following change and transformation's afterward, there must be grace different from the old—this is why worthy sages thereby display heaven's mandate.
translations.idiomatic: After great change must come a mercy unlike the old—that is how sages show heaven's mandate.

sentences.zh: 往者，昭帝即世而無嗣，大臣憂戚，焦心合謀，皆以昌邑尊親，援而立之。

translations.literal: In former times when Emperor Zhao went to the world without heir, great ministers worried and grieved, burned heart and joined plots, all because Changyi was honored and close, drew him and established him.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhao died without an heir, the ministers agonized and together raised Changyi because he was of the imperial kin.

sentences.zh: 然天不授命，淫亂其心，遂以自亡。

translations.literal: Yet heaven did not confer mandate; debauchery and disorder his heart, thereupon thereby self-perished.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven refused him: lust and folly mastered him, and he destroyed himself.

sentences.zh: 深察禍變之故，乃皇天之所以開至聖也。

translations.literal: Deeply examining misfortune and change's causes, then is how august heaven opens the utmost sage.
translations.idiomatic: Sound the reasons for that fall, and you see how High Heaven clears the path for the greatest sage.

sentences.zh: 故大將軍受命武帝，股肱漢國，披肝膽，決大計，黜亡義，立有德，輔天而行，然後宗廟以安，天下咸寧。

translations.literal: Therefore the great general received mandate from Emperor Wu, was thigh and arm to Han state, spread liver and gall, decided great plans, deposed the unrighteous, established the virtuous, assisted heaven in acting—only then the ancestral temple thereby was secure, the empire all was tranquil.
translations.idiomatic: The great general, charged by Emperor Wu, was pillar of Han: he laid bare his heart, set the great design, cast out the unworthy, raised the worthy, and acted with heaven—only then were the ancestral shrines secured and the realm quieted.

sentences.zh: 巨聞《春秋》正即位，大一統而慎始也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that the Spring and Autumn rectifies taking the throne, unifies the great unity and is careful at the beginning.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn teaches right succession, one rule for the realm, and a scrupulous beginning.

sentences.zh: 陛下初登至尊，與天合符，宜改前世之失，正始受之統，滌煩文，除民疾，存亡繼絕，以應天意。

translations.literal: Your Majesty at first ascended the utmost honor, matches heaven's tallies, ought to reform the former age's faults, rectify the beginning and received succession, wash away vexing documents, remove the people's ills, preserve the perishing and continue the cut off, to answer heaven's mind.
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty has just mounted the throne in accord with heaven: you should mend the errors of the past, set right the line you inherit, cut red tape, heal the people's hurts, restore broken lines, and answer heaven's will.

sentences.zh: 臣聞秦有十失，其一尚存，治獄之吏是也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that Qin had ten faults, one still survives—the clerks who govern prisons are it.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that Qin had ten faults; one remains—the way we run our prisons.

sentences.zh: 秦之時，羞文學，好武勇，賤仁義之士，貴治獄之吏；

translations.literal: In Qin's time, they shamed literary learning, loved martial courage, despised benevolent and righteous scholars, honored clerks who governed prisons.
translations.idiomatic: Under Qin, letters were despised, arms exalted, humane scholars slighted, and jailers honored.

sentences.zh: 正言者謂之誹謗，遏過者謂之妖言。

translations.literal: Straight speech they called slander; stopping faults they called prodigious speech.
translations.idiomatic: Plain truth was branded slander; honest criticism was called witch talk.

sentences.zh: 故盛服先生不用於世，忠良切言皆鬱於胸，譽諛之聲日滿於耳；

translations.literal: Therefore full-robed gentlemen were not used in the generation; loyal and good cutting words all were blocked in the breast; sounds of praise and fawning daily filled the ears.
translations.idiomatic: So scholars in court dress found no employment, loyal counsel choked in the throat, while flattery rang in every ear.

sentences.zh: 虛美熏心，實禍蔽塞。

translations.literal: Empty beauty smoked the heart; real calamity blocked and plugged.
translations.idiomatic: Hollow praise blinded the heart while true disaster was walled out.

sentences.zh: 此乃秦之所以亡天下也。

translations.literal: This is why Qin lost the empire.
translations.idiomatic: That is how Qin lost the realm.

sentences.zh: 方今天下賴陛下恩厚，亡金革之危，饑寒之患，父子夫妻戮力安家，然太平未洽者，獄亂之也。

translations.literal: Now the empire relies on Your Majesty's grace and thickness, lacks metal-and-leather danger, hunger and cold's calamity, father and son and husband and wife join strength to settle the household—yet great peace is not everywhere reached because prisons disorder it.
translations.idiomatic: Today, thanks to your kindness, we have no war or famine, and families work in peace—yet full peace eludes us because the prisons are in chaos.

sentences.zh: 夫獄者，天下之大命也，死者不可復生，絕者不可復屬。

translations.literal: As for prisons, they are the empire's great fate; the dead cannot again live, the cut off cannot again be joined.
translations.idiomatic: Justice is the empire's lifeline: the executed cannot rise, the mutilated cannot be made whole.

sentences.zh: 《書》曰：「與其殺不辜，寧失不經。」

translations.literal: The Documents say: "Rather than kill the innocent, better miss and violate the norm."
translations.idiomatic: The Documents say, "Better to risk mishandling a case than to put the innocent to death."

sentences.zh: 今治獄吏則不然，上下相驅，以刻為明；

translations.literal: Now clerks who govern prisons are not so; high and low drive one another, take harshness as clarity.
translations.idiomatic: Today's jailers do the opposite: rank drives rank and cruelty passes for competence.

sentences.zh: 深者獲公名，平者多後患。

translations.literal: The deep obtain fair name; the even meet later calamity.
translations.idiomatic: The harsh win a name for zeal; the fair inherit later trouble.

sentences.zh: 故治獄之吏皆欲人死，非憎人也，自安之道在人之死。

translations.literal: Therefore clerks who govern prisons all desire men to die—not that they hate men; the way to secure themselves lies in men's death.
translations.idiomatic: So every jailer wants a capital verdict—not from hatred, but because his own safety lies in another man's death.

sentences.zh: 是以死人之血流離於市，被刑之徒比肩而立，大辟之計歲以萬數，此仁聖之所以傷也。

translations.literal: Therefore dead men's blood flows and drifts in the market; tattooed convicts stand shoulder to shoulder; great executions' reckoning yearly uses ten thousand counts—this is why benevolent sages are wounded.
translations.idiomatic: Blood runs in the marketplace, the branded stand shoulder to shoulder, and executions mount by the tens of thousands each year—it wounds every humane ruler.

sentences.zh: 太平之未洽，凡以此也。

translations.literal: That great peace is not everywhere reached is all because of this.
translations.idiomatic: That is why perfect peace has not come.

sentences.zh: 夫人情安則樂生，痛則思死。

translations.literal: As for human feeling, when at ease then delights in living; in pain then thinks of death.
translations.idiomatic: Human nature seeks life when treated gently and thinks of death when tortured.

sentences.zh: 棰楚之下，何求而不得？

translations.literal: Beneath the rod and thorn, what seeking and not obtaining?
translations.idiomatic: Under the lash, what confession can fail?

sentences.zh: 故囚人不勝痛，則飾辭以視之；

translations.literal: Therefore the prisoner not bearing pain, then ornaments words to show the clerk.
translations.idiomatic: So the accused, unable to bear pain, frames a tale for the torturer.

sentences.zh: 吏治者利其然，則指道以明之；

translations.literal: The clerk who governs profits by its being so, then points the way to clarify it.
translations.idiomatic: The jailer, liking that outcome, coaches the story into shape.

sentences.zh: 上奏畏卻，則鍛練而周內之。

translations.literal: When submitting memorial fears rejection, then forges and tempers and completes the inner guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing a rebuff upstairs, he forges the record until the charge fits.

sentences.zh: 蓋奏當之成，雖咎繇聽之，猶以為死有餘辜。

translations.literal: When the memorial on the fitting sentence is complete, even if Gao Yao heard it, still he would think death left surplus guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Once the sentencing memorial is on file, even Gao Yao presiding would conclude the prisoner deserved worse than death.

sentences.zh: 何則？

translations.literal: Why was it?
translations.idiomatic: Why?

sentences.zh: 成練者眾，文致之罪明也。

translations.literal: Those who perfect the forged case are many; the crime wrought by documents is clear.
translations.idiomatic: Too many hands have polished the forged case, and the paper "crime" reads as plain as day.

sentences.zh: 是以獄吏專為深刻，殘賊而亡極，偷為一切，不顧國患，此世之大賊也。

translations.literal: Therefore prison clerks specialize in being harsh and deep, cruelly plundering without limit, secretly doing catch-all measures, not caring for the state's calamity—these are the great robbers of the age.
translations.idiomatic: So jailers trade in cruelty and extortion without end, cut every corner, and never mind the harm to the state—they are the great brigands of our time.

sentences.zh: 故俗語曰：「畫地為獄，議不入；

translations.literal: Therefore common saying: "Drawing a prison on the ground, counsel does not enter."
translations.idiomatic: Hence the proverb: "They draw a jail on the dust—yet counsel never crosses the line."

sentences.zh: 刻木為吏，期不對。」

translations.literal: "Carving wood for a clerk, agreement not to answer."
translations.idiomatic: "They carve a wooden magistrate—yet no one will plead before it."

sentences.zh: 此皆疾吏之風，悲痛之辭也。

translations.literal: These all are the wind of hating clerks, words of grief and pain.
translations.idiomatic: Such words are the voice of a people who loathe their jailers and grieve their wrongs.

sentences.zh: 故天下之患，莫深於獄；

translations.literal: Therefore the empire's calamity, none is deeper than prisons.
translations.idiomatic: No scourge in the realm runs deeper than the prisons.

sentences.zh: 敗法亂正，離親塞道，莫甚乎治獄之吏。

translations.literal: Defeating law and disordering rectitude, parting kin and blocking the Way—none is worse than clerks who govern prisons.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing corrupts law, severs families, and chokes the Way like the men who run those halls.

sentences.zh: 此所謂一尚存者也。

translations.literal: This is what is called the one that still survives.
translations.idiomatic: That is the one of Qin's ten faults that still lives among us.

sentences.zh: 臣聞烏鳶之卵不毀，而後鳳凰集；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that if crow and kite eggs are not destroyed, afterward the phoenix gathers.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that when no one smashes the crows' and kites' eggs, the phoenix will come to roost.

sentences.zh: 誹謗之罪不誅，而後良言進。

translations.literal: If slander crimes are not executed, afterward good words advance.
translations.idiomatic: When no one dies for the crime of "slander," honest counsel will flow again.

sentences.zh: 故古人有言：「山藪藏疾，川澤納污，瑾瑜匿惡，國君含詬。」

translations.literal: Therefore the ancients had a saying: "Mountain thickets hide illness, rivers and marshes accept filth; fine jade conceals flaws, the state lord bears reproach."
translations.idiomatic: The ancients said, "Hills hide sickness, rivers hold muck; flawless jade may bear a blemish, and a true king may bear a slur."

sentences.zh: 唯陛下除誹謗以招切言，開天下之口，廣箴諫之路，掃亡秦之失，尊文、武之德，省法制，寬刑罰，以廢治獄，則太平之風可興於世，永履和樂，與天亡極，天下幸甚。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty abolish the crime of slander to summon cutting words, open the empire's mouths, widen the path of needle remonstrance, sweep away perished Qin's faults, honor Wen and Wu's virtue, reduce the legal system, lighten punishments and penalties, in order to cast off prison-driven justice—then the wind of great peace can rise in the age, forever tread harmony and joy, with heaven without limit; the empire would be greatly fortunate.
translations.idiomatic: If you strike down the law against slander, open every mouth to frank advice, widen the road of remonstrance, cast off Qin's errors, lift up the model of Kings Wen and Wu, simplify statutes and ease punishments, and break the jailers' grip on justice, then the breeze of true peace will stir in our time, harmony will last as long as heaven, and the realm will know a great blessing.

sentences.zh: 上善其言，遷廣陽私府長。

translations.literal: The emperor approved his words, moved him to chief of the Guangyang private treasury.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor praised his memorial and promoted him to steward of the Guangyang princely treasury.

sentences.zh: 內史舉溫舒文學高第，遷右扶風丞。

translations.literal: The metropolitan governor recommended Wenshu's literary learning as high grade, moved him to assistant governor of Youfufeng.
translations.idiomatic: The metropolitan governor ranked him top in the literary examination, and he was promoted to assistant governor of Youfufeng.

sentences.zh: 時，詔書令公卿選可使匈奴者。

translations.literal: At that time an edict ordered dukes and ministers to select who could be sent to the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: An edict then called on high officials to name envoys fit for the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 溫舒上書，願給廝養，暴骨方對，以盡臣節。

translations.literal: Wenshu submitted a memorial, desiring to supply menial nurture, expose his bones on the frontier in requital, in order to exhaust your servant's duty.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Wenshu offered himself as a menial groom, begging to leave his bones bleaching on the frontier if that would fulfill a subject's duty.

sentences.zh: 事下度遼將軍范明友、太僕杜延年問狀，罷歸故官。

translations.literal: The matter was sent down to the General Who Crosses the Liao Fan Mingyou and the Chamberlain Du Yannian to inquire the situation; he was dismissed and returned to his former office.
translations.idiomatic: The case went to General Fan Mingyou and Chamberlain Du Yannian for review; he was sent back to his old post.

sentences.zh: 久之，遷臨淮太守，治有異跡，卒於官。

translations.literal: After a long time, he was moved to grand administrator of Linhuai; his governance had unusual traces; he died in office.
translations.idiomatic: Later he became grand administrator of Linhuai, where his rule left a remarkable record, and he died in harness.

sentences.zh: 溫舒從祖父受歷數天文，以為漢厄三七之間，上封事以豫戒。

translations.literal: Wenshu from his great-uncle received calendar reckoning and astronomy, holding that Han's misfortune fell in the three-seven interval, and submitted sealed memorials to forewarn.
translations.idiomatic: He learned calendrical astronomy from a great-uncle, believed Han would meet crisis in a "three times seven" cycle, and filed sealed warnings.

sentences.zh: 成帝時，谷永亦言如此。

translations.literal: At the time of Emperor Cheng, Gu Yong also spoke thus.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Cheng, Gu Yong voiced the same prophecy.

sentences.zh: 及王莽篡位，欲章代漢之符，著其語焉。

translations.literal: When Wang Mang usurped the throne, wishing to display omens of replacing Han, he recorded these words therein.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang seized the throne and sought omens of Han's supersession, he set these sayings down in his propaganda.

sentences.zh: 溫舒子及孫皆至牧守大官。

translations.literal: Wenshu's sons and grandsons all reached shepherd and grand office.
translations.idiomatic: His sons and grandsons rose to governorships and other high posts.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：春秋魯臧孫達以禮諫君，君子以為有後。

translations.literal: Appraisal says: In Spring and Autumn Lu's Zang Sunda used ritual to remonstrate with the lord; the gentleman thought him one who had posterity.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: In the Spring and Autumn Annals, Zang Sunda of Lu remonstrated with ritual—the gentleman said his line would endure.

sentences.zh: 賈山自下劘上，鄒陽、枚乘游於危國，然卒免刑戮者，以其言正也。

translations.literal: Jia Shan from below admonished above; Zou Yang and Mei Sheng roamed in perilous states, yet in the end escaped execution and mutilation—because their words were correct.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Shan counseled from the bottom of society upward; Zou Yang and Mei Sheng served treasonous courts, yet all escaped the block—because their counsel was just.

sentences.zh: 路溫舒辭順而意篤，遂為世家，宜哉！

translations.literal: Lu Wenshu's phrases were compliant and his intent sincere, thereupon his house became an established clan—fitting indeed!
translations.idiomatic: Lu Wenshu spoke with deference yet meant every word, and his family rose to lasting prominence—exactly as he deserved.
